1988.assessment:
Post Iran-Iraq War. Iraq emerged with massive debt burden estimated
at $80-100 billion, mostly to Gulf states and Western creditors. Debt
to GDP ratio extremely high. Government finances devastated by 8-year
war. Fiscal situation unsustainable.

1989.assessment:
Continued debt crisis. Iraq unable to service war debts. Tensions with
Kuwait over debt forgiveness and oil policy. Government revenues from
oil exports insufficient to cover debt service and reconstruction needs.

1990.assessment:
August 1990: Kuwait invasion. UN sanctions imposed August 6. Government
finances collapse as oil revenues halt. Debt service impossible. Fiscal
situation deteriorates from unsustainable debt to complete collapse.

1991.assessment:
Gulf War. Comprehensive UN sanctions. No oil revenue. Government finances
completely collapsed. Printing money to cover expenses leads to hyperinflation.
War reparations imposed. Debt service zero. Fiscal catastrophe.

1992.assessment:
Sanctions continue. No oil revenue. Government finances via currency
printing. Hyperinflation accelerating. Debt unpayable and growing due
to interest accumulation and reparations. Fiscal collapse.

1993.assessment:
Peak hyperinflation period. Currency worthless. Government finances
completely collapsed. Debt meaningless in context of non-functioning
fiscal system. Complete fiscal breakdown.

1994.assessment:
Sanctions period. No conventional government revenue. Fiscal system
non-functional. Debt accumulating but no payment mechanism or tracking.
Complete fiscal collapse continues.

1995.assessment:
Pre Oil-for-Food. Government finances collapsed. Debt unpayable and
untracked. Fiscal situation catastrophic. Sanctions prevent any normal
fiscal operations.

1996.assessment:
Oil-for-Food begins December 1996. First oil revenue since 1990 but
controlled by UN for humanitarian purposes. Government fiscal situation
slightly improved but debt burden unchanged. Still fiscal collapse but
humanitarian relief begins.

1997.assessment:
Oil-for-Food operational. Some government revenue from oil but heavily
controlled. Debt service still impossible. Debt burden estimated over
100% of GDP. Fiscal collapse continues despite Oil-for-Food relief.

1998.assessment:
Oil-for-Food volumes increasing. Government receives some revenue but
insufficient for normal fiscal operations. Debt burden remains unsustainable.
Fiscal situation stabilized from collapse but still crisis level.

1999.assessment:
Oil-for-Food mature. Higher oil prices increase revenues. Debt estimated
$120-140 billion. No debt service. Fiscal situation remains crisis level
but no longer complete collapse due to Oil-for-Food revenues.

2000.assessment:
Late Saddam era. Oil-for-Food plus smuggling provide revenues. Debt
burden massive but debt service non-existent. Fiscal crisis continues.
Sanctions weakening but government finances remain unsustainable.

2001.assessment:
Pre-invasion. Debt estimated $120+ billion. Oil-for-Food and illicit
revenues provide government income. Debt service zero. Fiscal crisis
continues. Government manipulates Oil-for-Food for revenue.

2002.assessment:
Final pre-invasion year. Debt burden unchanged. Fiscal situation crisis
level. Uncertainty about impending conflict prevents any debt negotiations.
Government finances from Oil-for-Food and sanctions evasion.

2003.assessment:
US invasion March 2003. Saddam regime falls. Iraq's debt estimated
$120-140 billion against collapsed GDP. Debt to GDP ratio ~400-500%.
CPA begins debt relief negotiations. Paris Club process initiated.
Fiscal crisis extreme but debt relief process beginning.

sources.citation: World Bank Iraq debt relief analysis

2004.assessment:
CPA period. Paris Club agrees November 2004 to 80% debt relief in stages.
Initial debt relief begins. GDP recovering slightly. Debt burden still
massive but relief process underway. Fiscal situation improving from
complete collapse but still crisis level.

2005.assessment:
First stage Paris Club debt relief implemented. Debt burden declining
rapidly. Oil revenues increasing. Budget deficits moderate due to US
reconstruction spending and oil revenue. Fiscal situation dramatically
improved but still elevated debt.

2006.assessment:
Continued debt relief. Second stage Paris Club reduction. Oil revenues
high despite civil war. Budget situation improving. Debt declining toward
manageable levels. Fiscal health much improved from 2003-2004 crisis.

2007.assessment:
Final Paris Club debt relief stage. Debt burden reduced to sustainable
levels. Oil boom year. Budget surpluses emerging. Fiscal situation
transformed from crisis to manageable despite peak violence.

2008.assessment:
Oil boom year (prices to $140/barrel). Massive budget surpluses. Debt
rapidly declining. Fiscal health excellent. Government accumulates
reserves. Debt burden now manageable and declining.

2009.assessment:
Oil prices recover from 2008 crash. Budget deficits moderate due to
expanded spending. Debt stable at manageable levels. Fiscal health good.
Government builds financial reserves.

2010.assessment:
Oil revenues high. Government spending increases. Small deficits but
debt remains manageable. Fiscal health good. Political deadlock delays
budget but oil revenues sustain government operations.

2011.assessment:
Oil production expanding. Revenues high. Budget deficits small. Debt
declining. Fiscal health good. US withdrawal reduces some costs. Government
finances sustainable.

2012.assessment:
Oil production increases. High revenues. Small budget deficits. Debt
declining to sustainable levels. Fiscal health strong. Government
accumulates reserves.

2013.assessment:
Oil revenues high. Government spending increases but revenues keep pace.
Debt low. Fiscal health good. Budget surpluses in some quarters. Fiscal
position sustainable.

2014.assessment:
June 2014: ISIS crisis. Oil price collapse second half. Budget deficits
surge. War spending increases. Revenues collapse. Debt rising. Fiscal
crisis beginning. Government forced to borrow.

2015.assessment:
Full fiscal crisis. Oil revenues collapsed. War spending high. Massive
budget deficits. Government borrowing heavily. Salary delays. Debt
surging. Fiscal situation deteriorating rapidly.

2016.assessment:
Continued fiscal crisis. Low oil prices. War spending. Massive deficits.
Debt approaching crisis levels. Government austerity measures. IMF
assistance program. Fiscal sustainability threatened.

2017.assessment:
ISIS territorial defeat July 2017. Oil prices recovering. Deficits
declining. Debt stabilizing. Fiscal crisis easing. IMF program continues.
Fiscal situation improving but debt still elevated.

2018.assessment:
Oil prices higher. Revenues recovering. Deficits declining. Debt
declining from peak. Fiscal health improving. Post-ISIS reconstruction
needs but revenue growth allows. Fiscal situation stabilizing.

2019.assessment:
Oil revenues stable. Deficits moderate. Debt declining. Fiscal health
improving. October protests over government spending and corruption.
Budget implementation challenged by political crisis but fiscal position
manageable.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 pandemic. Oil price collapse (negative prices April 2020).
Revenue catastrophe. Massive deficits. Salary delays. Emergency borrowing.
Debt surges. Fiscal crisis. IMF emergency assistance. White Paper reform
plan announced.

2021.assessment:
Oil prices recovering. Revenues increasing. Deficits declining. Debt
stabilizing. Fiscal crisis easing. Political deadlock delays reforms
but oil recovery provides fiscal breathing room.

2022.assessment:
Oil boom from Russia-Ukraine war. Revenue windfall. Budget surpluses.
Debt declining rapidly. Fiscal health dramatically improved. Government
accumulates reserves. Fiscal position strongest since 2013.

2023.assessment:
Oil revenues high. Budget surpluses. Debt declining. Fiscal health
strong. Sudani government focuses on spending on services and infrastructure.
Fiscal position sustainable. Reserves growing.

2024.assessment:
Oil revenues remain elevated. Budget likely balanced or small surplus.
Debt declining. Fiscal health good. Government spending on infrastructure
and services. Fiscal position sustainable barring oil price collapse.

2025.assessment:
Current year. Fiscal trajectory dependent on oil prices. Structural
dependence on oil revenue unchanged. Fiscal health good if oil prices
stable. Vulnerability to oil price shocks remains Iraq's fundamental
fiscal challenge.

1988.assessment:
End of Iran-Iraq war. Economy devastated by 8 years of conflict but
oil revenues still flowing. GDP recovering from wartime lows.

1989.assessment:
Post-war reconstruction efforts. Oil production recovering.
Economy stabilizing after Iran-Iraq war.

1990.assessment:
Kuwait invasion in August 1990. UN sanctions imposed immediately.
Oil exports halted. Economy begins severe contraction.

1991.assessment:
Gulf War devastation. Coalition bombing destroyed infrastructure.
Comprehensive UN sanctions in effect. GDP collapsed to fraction of pre-war level.

sources.citation: CESR - The Human Costs of War in Iraq

1992.assessment:
Continued sanctions. Infrastructure remains destroyed.
Hyperinflation beginning. Living standards plummeting.

1993.assessment:
Sanctions devastate economy. Oil-for-Food program not yet in place.
Informal economy and smuggling grow as formal economy collapses.

1994.assessment:
Economy remains at rock bottom. Currency collapsed.
Middle class impoverished. Public sector wages worthless.

1995.assessment:
UN Security Council Resolution 986 passed (Oil-for-Food) but not yet
implemented. Economy remains devastated.

1996.assessment:
Oil-for-Food program begins implementation. First oil exports
in December 1996. Slight economic improvement beginning.

1997.assessment:
Oil-for-Food program expanding. Oil revenues used for food and
medicine imports. Some economic improvement but still far below pre-1990.

1998.assessment:
Oil-for-Food program fully operational. Operation Desert Fox
bombing in December. Economy slowly recovering within sanctions framework.

1999.assessment:
Continued slow recovery under Oil-for-Food. Oil prices rising.
Infrastructure still degraded. Brain drain accelerating.

2000.assessment:
Oil prices high. Economy stabilized at low level. Sanctions fatigue
internationally. Smart sanctions debate begins.

2001.assessment:
Economy under continued sanctions. Post-9/11 international focus
shifts toward Iraq. Oil smuggling continues outside UN framework.

2002.assessment:
Invasion preparations create uncertainty. Oil-for-Food continues.
Economy stagnant under sanctions. Weapons inspectors return.

2003.assessment:
US invasion March 2003. Saddam regime falls. Massive infrastructure
destruction and looting. Oil production halted then slowly restarting.
CPA takes over economic management. Sanctions lifted May 2003.

2004.assessment:
Post-invasion reconstruction begins. Oil production recovering.
Insurgency disrupting economic activity. CPA hands over to interim government.
Large international aid flows.

2005.assessment:
Elections held. Oil production increasing. Insurgency intensifying.
Sectarian tensions rising. Economy growing despite security challenges.

2006.assessment:
Sectarian civil war erupts after Samarra mosque bombing.
Economy functioning in some areas despite extreme violence.
Oil revenues rising with global prices.

2007.assessment:
US troop surge. Violence peaks then begins declining later in year.
Oil prices rising sharply. Economic growth despite ongoing conflict.

2008.assessment:
Violence significantly reduced. Oil prices peak mid-year then crash.
Government launching major reconstruction programs.
Economy recovering as security improves.

2009.assessment:
Global financial crisis and lower oil prices affect revenues.
Security continuing to improve. US forces beginning withdrawal.

2010.assessment:
Elections produce prolonged political deadlock. Oil production
expanding with international investment. Security relatively stable.

2011.assessment:
US forces complete withdrawal. Oil production ramping up.
High oil prices boost revenues. Arab Spring does not significantly affect Iraq.

2012.assessment:
Strong economic growth. Oil production at highest levels since 1980s.
Political tensions between Maliki government and Sunnis growing.

2013.assessment:
Economic peak. Violence rising in Anbar province. ISIS beginning
to emerge. Oil production strong. Political crisis with Sunni protests.

2014.assessment:
ISIS captures Mosul in June. Massive territorial losses. Oil prices
begin crashing. Military spending surges. Economy contracts despite
southern oil production continuing.

2015.assessment:
Full impact of oil price crash. ISIS war costs enormous. Budget
crisis forces austerity. Economy contracts sharply.

2016.assessment:
Continued fiscal crisis. Mosul operation launched. Oil production
actually increasing to compensate for low prices. IMF program begun.

2017.assessment:
ISIS defeated militarily. Mosul liberated but destroyed.
Reconstruction needs massive. Oil prices recovering.
Kurdistan independence referendum creates tensions.

2018.assessment:
Post-ISIS recovery. Elections held. Reconstruction efforts ongoing.
Oil prices higher. Abdul-Mahdi becomes PM after prolonged negotiations.

2019.assessment:
October protests erupt demanding reform. PM Abdul-Mahdi resigns.
Economy stable but public anger over corruption and unemployment.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 pandemic and oil price crash create twin crisis.
GDP contracts sharply. Currency devaluation (December).
Kadhimi government struggles with fiscal crisis.

2021.assessment:
Recovery from 2020 crash. Oil prices rising. Elections held in October.
Currency remains devalued. Inflation pressures from devaluation.

2022.assessment:
Oil price surge due to Ukraine war boosts revenues. Political deadlock
for most of year after Sadrist bloc withdraws. Sudani government
formed in October. Highest GDP per capita on record.

2023.assessment:
Oil prices moderate from 2022 peaks. Sudani government implementing
budget and development plans. Currency pressure from dollar shortage.
Dinar trading at premium on parallel market.

2024.assessment:
Economy relatively stable. Oil production steady under OPEC+ quotas.
Development Road project announced. Banking sector reforms ongoing.

2025.assessment: Estimated 2025 GDP per capita. Iraq oil revenues continuing; political instability; reconstruction ongoing. IMF projects modest growth. Estimated ~$6000-6500 USD per capita based on 2024 trajectory.

1988.assessment:
Post-Iran-Iraq war period. Economy stressed but not yet in crisis.
Government controlling prices through subsidies and rationing.

1989.assessment:
Relative stabilization after war. Oil revenues helping control prices.
Subsidies maintaining basic goods affordability.

1990.assessment:
Kuwait invasion and immediate sanctions begin disrupting economy.
Inflation rising as imports restricted and shortages develop.

1991.assessment:
Gulf War devastation. Currency collapses. Hyperinflation begins.
Official statistics meaningless as informal economy dominates.

sources.citation: CESR - The Human Costs of War in Iraq

1992.assessment:
Hyperinflation continues. Dinar loses most of its value.
Multiple exchange rates emerge. Barter economy grows.

1993.assessment:
Peak hyperinflation period. Government salaries become worthless.
Middle class wealth destroyed. Parallel dollar economy emerges.

1994.assessment:
Currency stabilizes at very low level after massive devaluation.
Inflation remains extremely high but hyperinflation phase ending.

1995.assessment:
High but stabilizing inflation. Economy has adjusted to sanctions.
Dual economy with official and black market prices.

1996.assessment:
Oil-for-Food begins bringing some stability. Inflation remains high
but improving. Basic goods more available through UN program.

1997.assessment:
Oil-for-Food expanding. Price stability improving in sectors covered
by UN program. Overall inflation still elevated.

1998.assessment:
Relative price stability under Oil-for-Food framework.
Government maintaining subsidies where possible.

1999.assessment:
Oil prices rising. Economy stabilized at low level.
Inflation moderate by Iraqi standards of the 1990s.

2000.assessment:
Low official inflation as economy stabilized under sanctions.
High oil prices help government finances.

2001.assessment:
Relatively stable prices. Oil-for-Food functioning.
Economy adjusted to long-term sanctions.

2002.assessment:
Uncertainty about possible war driving price increases.
Stockpiling behavior affecting markets.

2003.assessment:
Invasion disrupts economy. Looting and destruction.
CPA introduces new dinar in October. Price volatility extreme.

2004.assessment:
High inflation as reconstruction begins. New currency stabilizing.
Subsidies being reformed. Import prices rising.

2005.assessment:
High inflation continues. Fuel subsidy reforms causing price spikes.
Security situation affecting supply chains and prices.

2006.assessment:
Sectarian civil war. Major fuel subsidy reduction. Inflation spikes.
Central Bank struggling to control prices amid violence.

2007.assessment:
Inflation declining from 2006 peak. Central Bank tightening policy.
Security improving late in year helping supply chains.

2008.assessment:
Major disinflation. Central Bank policy success. Dinar appreciating.
Security improvements helping normalize markets.

2009.assessment:
Deflation as global financial crisis hits oil prices.
Dinar stable. Government spending reduced.

2010.assessment: Low inflation. Price stability achieved. Central Bank credibility established.

2011.assessment:
Moderate inflation. Global commodity prices rising.
Strong government spending from oil revenues.

2012.assessment: Moderate inflation. Economy growing strongly. Demand-pull pressures.

2013.assessment: Low inflation. Price stability maintained. Central Bank effective.

2014.assessment:
Low inflation despite ISIS crisis. Central Bank maintains stability.
Northern areas under ISIS have different economic conditions.

2015.assessment:
Very low inflation. Oil price crash reducing demand. Austerity measures.
Central Bank defending exchange rate.

2016.assessment:
Near-zero inflation. Fiscal austerity. Weak demand.
Exchange rate stable despite budget pressures.

2017.assessment:
Price stability. Post-ISIS reconstruction not yet generating inflation.
Exchange rate maintained.

2018.assessment:
Very low inflation. Exchange rate anchor working effectively.
Economy recovering but without price pressures.

2019.assessment:
Slight deflation. October protests not affecting prices significantly.
Exchange rate stable.

2020.assessment:
Low inflation during COVID crisis. Currency devaluation in December
not fully reflected in annual average. Deflation pressures from weak demand.

2021.assessment:
Inflation rises due to December 2020 currency devaluation.
Import prices higher. Food and fuel costs increasing.

2022.assessment:
Moderate inflation. Global food and energy prices high due to Ukraine war.
Government subsidies limiting domestic impact.

2023.assessment:
Inflation moderating. Currency pressure from dollar restrictions.
Parallel market rate higher than official rate.

2024.assessment:
Moderate inflation expected. Banking sector reforms affecting dollar access.
Subsidies helping contain prices.

2025.assessment: Estimated 2025 inflation. Iraq CBI maintaining exchange rate peg. Moderate inflation continuing from 2024. Estimated ~4% based on recent trend.

1988.assessment:
Post Iran-Iraq War period. Iraq heavily oil-dependent. Trade constrained
by war debt and regional tensions. Oil exports to world market but imports
limited by debt burden. Trade openness moderate given oil dependence.

1989.assessment:
Continued oil-dependent trade. Exports recovering from war period. Trade
moderate but constrained by debt and international isolation.

1990.assessment:
August 1990: Kuwait invasion triggers UN sanctions (August 6). Trade
collapses rapidly. Oil exports halted under UN embargo. Imports restricted.
Transition from moderate trade to autarky.

1991.assessment:
Comprehensive UN sanctions. No legal oil exports. Trade essentially zero
except smuggling. Autarky conditions. Gulf War destruction. International
isolation complete.

1992.assessment:
Continued comprehensive sanctions. No legal oil exports. Minimal trade
through smuggling networks. Economic autarky. Humanitarian crisis deepens.

1993.assessment:
Sanctions continuing. Smuggling increases but remains small relative to
pre-sanctions trade. Autarky conditions. Hyperinflation era.

1994.assessment:
Continued autarky. Smuggling through Jordan, Turkey, Iran grows but
insufficient to restore normal trade patterns. Economy isolated.

1995.assessment:
Autarky continues. Oil-for-Food negotiations begin but not yet implemented.
Smuggling networks more organized but trade volumes minimal.

1996.assessment:
Oil-for-Food program begins December 1996. First legal oil exports since
1990. Humanitarian imports allowed. Trade still severely restricted but
no longer complete autarky. Transition year.

1997.assessment:
Oil-for-Food operational. Controlled oil exports for humanitarian imports.
Trade volumes increasing but still severely restricted. UN controls all
transactions. Trade openness minimal but improving from autarky.

1998.assessment:
Oil-for-Food volumes increase. Ceiling raised. Trade still restricted
to humanitarian goods. Smuggling continues alongside official channels.
Trade openness low but improving.

1999.assessment:
Oil-for-Food mature. Oil price recovery increases revenues. Trade volumes
higher but still restricted to permitted categories. Sanctions eroding
as countries open unofficial trade. Trade openness low.

2000.assessment:
Oil-for-Food continuing. Sanctions weakening as countries pursue bilateral
deals. Iraqi government manipulates program. Trade increasing but still
constrained. Smuggling substantial.

2001.assessment:
Sanctions erosion continues. Oil-for-Food volumes high. Smuggling networks
robust. Trade openness improving but still restricted. Multiple parallel
trade channels (legal Oil-for-Food, smuggling, illegal bilateral).

2002.assessment:
Final pre-invasion year. Oil-for-Food continues. Sanctions still formally
in place but widely evaded. Trade openness restricted but higher than
mid-1990s. Uncertainty about impending conflict.

2003.assessment:
US invasion March 2003. Regime change April. Sanctions lifted May 22.
Oil-for-Food ends November. Oil exports resume under CPA. Massive imports
for reconstruction and humanitarian needs. Trade surges as Iraq opens
completely. High trade openness due to reconstruction imports and oil exports.

2004.assessment:
CPA period. Oil exports recovering. Reconstruction imports massive.
Insurgency disrupts some trade routes. Trade openness high driven by
oil exports and reconstruction needs. Iraq fully integrated into global
economy but security challenges.

2005.assessment:
Post-CPA. Iraqi transitional government. Oil exports continue. Imports
remain high for reconstruction and consumption. Trade openness high.
Security situation affects land routes but sea trade continues.

2006.assessment:
Sectarian civil war begins. Oil exports continue despite violence.
Imports high. Trade openness very high relative to GDP. Security disrupts
internal distribution but external trade continues, especially oil exports.

2007.assessment:
Peak violence. US surge. Oil exports maintained. Import dependence high.
Trade openness high despite security crisis. Oil sector insulated from
much of the violence. Food and goods imports essential.

2008.assessment:
Violence declining. Oil boom year (prices reach $140/barrel mid-year).
Oil exports surge. Imports increase with higher revenues. Trade openness
very high. Iraq heavily oil-export dependent.

2009.assessment:
Oil prices recover from 2008 crash. Trade openness high. Iraq oil-dependent
economy fully integrated into global oil markets. Imports meet most
consumer needs. Minimal domestic manufacturing.

2010.assessment:
Trade openness high. Oil exports 95% of government revenue. Imports
supply most consumer goods. Trade pattern: oil exports, everything else
imports. Economy structurally dependent on trade.

2011.assessment:
US withdrawal December 2011. Oil exports continue expanding. Trade openness
high. Iraq increases oil production. Import dependence continues for food,
manufactured goods, services.

2012.assessment:
Oil production increases. Exports high. Trade openness very high. Iraq
among most trade-dependent economies globally due to oil monoculture
and import dependence. No economic diversification.

2013.assessment:
Trade openness remains very high. Oil exports dominant. Security deteriorating
but oil sector continues. Imports sustain consumption. Trade structure
unchanged: export oil, import everything else.

2014.assessment:
June 2014: ISIS seizes Mosul. Northern oil fields disrupted but southern
exports continue. Oil price collapse second half 2014. Trade volumes
decline. Trade openness still high but reduced from peak. Export revenue
crisis.

2015.assessment:
Low oil prices. Export revenues collapsed. Imports cut due to fiscal
crisis. Trade openness declining. Government austerity. Kurdistan region
disputes over oil. War against ISIS.

2016.assessment:
Continued low oil prices. Anti-ISIS campaign. Trade volumes suppressed
by fiscal crisis. Oil exports volumes increase but prices low. Import
compression continues. Trade openness moderate.

2017.assessment:
ISIS territorial defeat July 2017. Oil prices recovering. Oil exports
increase. Imports rise for reconstruction. Trade openness increasing.
Northern fields back online. Kurdistan crisis over oil.

2018.assessment:
Oil prices higher. Export revenues improving. Imports increase. Trade
openness high. Iraq remains oil monoculture. Diversification minimal
despite post-ISIS recovery.

2019.assessment:
Oil price volatility. Trade openness high but declining from 2018. October
protests disrupt some trade. Iraq remains oil-export dependent. Import
dependence unchanged.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 pandemic. Oil price collapse (negative prices April 2020).
Trade volumes crash. Export revenues devastated. Import compression.
Trade openness declines sharply. Fiscal crisis reduces imports.

2021.assessment:
Oil prices recovering. Export revenues increasing. Imports rise. Trade
openness rebounds from 2020 crash. Economic recovery driven by oil price
recovery and trade expansion.

2022.assessment:
Oil boom from Russia-Ukraine war. Oil prices surge. Export revenues
record levels. Imports surge with high revenues. Trade openness very
high. Iraq benefits massively from oil price shock.

2023.assessment:
Oil prices stable at elevated levels. Trade openness very high. Iraq
remains one of most trade-dependent economies globally. Oil monoculture
unchanged. Import dependence total for manufactures, food.

2024.assessment:
Trade openness remains high. Oil dependence unchanged. Regional tensions
from Gaza/Israel conflict affect trade routes but oil exports continue.
Import dependence structural feature of Iraqi economy.

2025.assessment:
Current year. Trade trajectory dependent on oil prices and regional
stability. Structural oil dependence unchanged. Economic diversification
minimal despite two decades of stated policy goals.

1988.assessment:
Pre-Gulf War Iraq under Saddam Hussein's regime. State-dominated economy with
significant public sector employment. Unemployment relatively low due to large
state apparatus and military mobilization during Iran-Iraq War aftermath.

1989.assessment:
Continued state-dominated employment. Economy recovering from Iran-Iraq War.
Large public sector and military employment kept official unemployment low.

1990.assessment:
August 1990 invasion of Kuwait. Economic disruption begins. Employment still
maintained through state apparatus but economic shock beginning.

sources.citation: ILO reports on Iraq labor market

1991.assessment:
Gulf War and imposition of comprehensive UN sanctions. Massive economic collapse.
Private sector devastated, state unable to maintain employment levels.
Unemployment surges dramatically.

sources.citation: ILO: Impact of UN sanctions on Iraqi labor market

1992.assessment:
Full sanctions in effect. Economic activity severely constrained. Unemployment
remains very high as formal economy shrinks. Growth of informal sector.

1993.assessment:
Continued sanctions. Unemployment remains very high. Public sector wages
become worthless due to hyperinflation. Many nominally employed but
functionally unemployed.

sources.citation: UN FAO reports on Iraq humanitarian situation

1994.assessment:
Peak sanctions period. Economy at its worst. Very high unemployment and
underemployment. Formal sector minimal outside government.

1995.assessment:
Continued very high unemployment. Oil-for-Food program not yet implemented.
Informal economy dominates. Public sector employment nominal.

1996.assessment:
Oil-for-Food program begins (December 1996). Slight economic activity increase
but unemployment remains very high. Limited improvement in formal employment.

1997.assessment:
Oil-for-Food program operational. Some economic relief but unemployment
remains very high. Public sector dominant but wages insufficient.

1998.assessment:
Continued high unemployment despite Oil-for-Food. Economic activity constrained
by sanctions. Informal sector large but unmeasured.

1999.assessment:
Sanctions continuing. Unemployment high but somewhat stabilized as economy
adapts to constrained conditions through informal mechanisms.

2000.assessment:
Final years of Saddam regime. Sanctions erosion begins as countries open
trade. Some economic activity increase but unemployment remains high.

2001.assessment:
Pre-invasion period. Sanctions weakening. Iraqi government smuggling operations
and Oil-for-Food manipulation provide some economic activity but unemployment
remains high.

2002.assessment:
Final year before invasion. Economic uncertainty. Sanctions still in place
but weakening. Unemployment high as regime prepares for potential conflict.

2003.assessment:
US invasion March 2003. Saddam regime falls April. Complete economic
collapse. State institutions dissolve. CPA disbands Iraqi army (400,000
soldiers unemployed). Looting and lawlessness. Unemployment surges to
crisis levels. No formal economy outside Coalition operations.

2004.assessment:
CPA occupation continues. Insurgency intensifies. Unemployment remains
at crisis levels despite reconstruction spending. Security situation
prevents economic recovery. Public sector slowly reconstitutes.

2005.assessment:
Post-CPA period. Iraqi transitional government. Reconstruction spending
creates some employment but sectarian tensions rising. Security private
sector employs many. Unemployment high but declining from 2003-2004 peak.

2006.assessment:
Sectarian civil war begins. February 2006 Samarra mosque bombing.
Unemployment remains high. Security sector employment grows. Formal
private sector minimal. Displacement disrupts labor markets.

2007.assessment:
Peak violence year. US surge begins. Unemployment remains high despite
massive US spending. Sectarian conflict disrupts employment. "Sons of Iraq"
program employs 100,000 in security.

2008.assessment:
Violence declining. Surge working. Oil prices high providing revenue
for government employment. Unemployment begins declining. Public sector
expands. Formal private sector still weak.

2009.assessment:
Relative stability. Oil revenues fund public sector expansion. Unemployment
slowly declining but remains high. Youth unemployment particularly severe.
Private sector development limited.

2010.assessment:
Post-election political deadlock. Government formation delayed. Unemployment
stagnant at high levels. Oil sector expansion creates few jobs. Public
sector remains dominant employer.

2011.assessment:
US withdrawal completed December 2011. Arab Spring protests. Unemployment
remains high. Youth unemployment fuels protests. Public sector saturated.
Private sector weak.

2012.assessment:
Sectarian tensions rising. Syrian civil war spillover begins. Unemployment
stagnant. ISIS precursors gaining strength in Anbar. Economic stagnation
despite oil revenues.

2013.assessment:
Security deteriorating. ISIS emerging in western Iraq. Unemployment
remains stubbornly high. Anbar unrest. Government employment patronage
system strained. Youth unemployment crisis.

2014.assessment:
June 2014: ISIS seizes Mosul and large portions of northern and western
Iraq. Massive displacement. Economic activity collapses in ISIS areas.
Unemployment surges. 3 million displaced. Oil prices collapse in second
half. Fiscal crisis begins.

sources.citation: ILO: Impact of ISIS on Iraq labor market

2015.assessment:
Full-scale war against ISIS. Government controls limited territory.
Oil revenues collapsed. Fiscal crisis. Salary delays. Unemployment
understated due to displacement and non-counting of ISIS areas. Youth
unemployment over 40%.

2016.assessment:
Anti-ISIS campaign intensifies. Mosul liberation begins October 2016.
Unemployment officially unchanged but situation varies dramatically by
region. Government employment strained by fiscal crisis. Displacement
continues.

2017.assessment:
ISIS territorial defeat July 2017. Mosul liberated. Reconstruction
beginning. Unemployment declining as government services restore.
Massive destruction in liberated areas. Youth unemployment 25%.

2018.assessment:
Post-ISIS recovery. Oil prices higher. Government revenues improving.
Unemployment declining. Youth unemployment remains high (25-30%).
Reconstruction employment. Political protests over unemployment and
services begin in Basra.

2019.assessment:
October 2019: massive protests over unemployment, corruption, services.
Unemployment officially moderate but youth unemployment crisis drives
protests. Public sector employment dominates. Private sector minimal.
Government paralyzed.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 pandemic. Oil price collapse (briefly negative April 2020).
Fiscal crisis. Salary delays. Unemployment rises. Private sector
devastated. Youth unemployment over 30%. Economic contraction.

2021.assessment:
Oil prices recovering. Economic recovery begins. Unemployment declining
slightly. October 2021 elections. Political deadlock continues.
Youth unemployment remains crisis level (25-30%).

2022.assessment:
Oil boom due to Russia-Ukraine war. High oil prices. Government revenues
surge. Unemployment declining. Public sector expansion. Political crisis
throughout year (government formation delayed until October).

2023.assessment:
Continued oil revenue strength. Unemployment declining slowly. Sudani
government focuses on services and employment. Public sector still
dominates. Private sector development minimal. Youth unemployment 23-25%.

2024.assessment:
Unemployment continues gradual decline. Public sector employment expansion
continues. Oil revenues sustain government spending. Private sector
development slow. Youth unemployment remains structural issue (22-25%).

2025.assessment:
Current year. Economic trajectory dependent on oil prices and regional
stability. Structural unemployment remains challenge. Youth unemployment
crisis persists despite overall economic growth.

1988.assessment:
Iraq maintained normal diplomatic relations. UN member. Arab League member.
Embassies in major capitals. Recognition from most countries. Some tensions
with neighbors but diplomatically integrated.

1989.assessment:
Normal diplomatic relations maintained. Full UN member. Arab League member.
Embassy presence in most countries. Recognized government. Regional disputes
with Kuwait over borders and debt.

1990.assessment:
August 1990: Kuwait invasion triggers rapid diplomatic isolation. UN condemns
invasion. Many countries sever ties, close embassies. Arab League splits.
Transition from normal relations to significant isolation. Still recognized
but isolated.

1991.assessment:
Gulf War. Pariah status. Most Western and Gulf states sever diplomatic
ties. Few embassies remain in Baghdad. Iraq isolated from international
community. Arab League suspended Iraq. Recognized but isolated.

1992.assessment:
Pariah status continues. Minimal embassy presence in Baghdad. Iraq isolated
from Western and Gulf countries. Some relations with Russia, China, France.
Arab League suspension continues. International pariah.

1993.assessment:
Continued pariah status. Few foreign embassies operating. Iraq maintains
some ties with Russia, China, select Arab states. Western diplomatic
isolation complete. Arab League suspension.

1994.assessment:
Pariah status. Jordan maintains relations. Russia and France push for
sanctions relief but Iraq remains diplomatically isolated. Limited embassy
presence. Arab League suspension continues.

1995.assessment:
Pariah status maintained. Oil-for-Food negotiations but diplomatic isolation
continues. Few countries maintain full relations. Arab League suspension.
International pariah despite some sanctions debate.

1996.assessment:
Pariah status. Oil-for-Food begins but diplomatic isolation persists.
Some countries (Russia, France, China) increase engagement but Iraq remains
largely isolated. No normalization with West or Gulf states.

1997.assessment:
Significant isolation but erosion beginning. Russia, France, China push
for normalization. Some Arab states quietly re-engage. Still isolated
from West and Gulf. Not full pariah but significantly isolated.

1998.assessment:
Significant isolation. Operation Desert Fox strains relations but some
countries increase diplomatic engagement. Russia, France oppose US/UK
policy. Arab states divided. Isolation weakening but still substantial.

1999.assessment:
Significant isolation weakening. Many countries open informal ties. Arab
states re-engage. Flights resume to Baghdad. Still isolated from US and
close allies but diplomatic thaw with many countries.

2000.assessment:
Partial isolation. Many Arab states restore relations. Russia, France,
China full engagement. Flights to Baghdad resume. Still isolated from
US, UK, Gulf monarchies. Arab League readmits Iraq 2000. Partial normalization.

2001.assessment:
Partial isolation. Post-9/11 increases US-Iraq tensions. Many countries
maintain relations despite US opposition. Russia, France, Arab states
engaged. Still isolated from US and close allies.

2002.assessment:
Partial isolation. Pre-invasion crisis. Resolution 1441 weapons inspections.
Diplomatic engagement from Russia, France, Germany opposing war. Arab
states divided. US/UK isolate Iraq but not universal isolation.

2003.assessment:
March 2003: Invasion. Regime change April. CPA assumes authority. Iraq
under occupation. Many countries refuse to recognize CPA as legitimate
government. Diplomatic limbo. Partial isolation during transition.

2004.assessment:
June 2004: Sovereignty transfer. Iraqi Interim Government. UN recognizes
new government. Countries begin opening embassies. Arab League welcomes
new government. Diplomatic normalization begins but insurgency creates
challenges.

sources.citation: UN recognition of Iraqi Interim Government

2005.assessment:
Iraqi Transitional Government. Elections January 2005. Full UN recognition.
Arab League engagement. Many countries reopen embassies. Diplomatic
normalization advancing despite security challenges. Normal relations
restored.

2006.assessment:
Permanent government. Full diplomatic relations. Many embassies operating
despite civil war violence. UN, Arab League, international organizations
fully engaged. Security limits some embassy operations but full diplomatic
recognition.

2007.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations maintained despite peak violence. Embassies
operate in heavily fortified conditions. International organizations
present. UN, Arab League engaged. Full recognition and integration despite
security crisis.

2008.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Security improving. More embassies open.
Arab League, UN, international engagement robust. Iraq fully integrated
into international diplomatic community. Normal relations.

2009.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Iraq hosts Arab League summit 2012 (later).
Embassy presence expanding. International organizations operating. Full
integration into international diplomatic community.

2010.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Political deadlock domestically but international
relations normal. Embassies operating. UN, Arab League, international
organizations engaged. Normal diplomatic status.

2011.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. US withdrawal December 2011. Iraq fully sovereign.
Arab League engagement. International organizations present. Normal diplomatic
integration maintained.

2012.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. March 2012: Iraq hosts Arab League summit
- symbolic return to Arab fold. Embassies operating. International engagement
robust. Normal diplomatic status.

2013.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Security deteriorating but diplomatic status
normal. Embassies operating. International organizations present. ISIS
emerging but diplomatic integration maintained.

2014.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. June 2014: ISIS crisis. International community
supports Iraqi government. Some embassy closures/evacuations for security
but diplomatic recognition and support continues. Normal relations with
minor disruptions.

2015.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Anti-ISIS coalition supports Iraq. International
engagement robust despite war. Embassies operating with security restrictions.
Full integration maintained.

2016.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Mosul liberation campaign. International
coalition present. Embassies operating. UN, Arab League engaged. Normal
diplomatic status maintained despite war.

2017.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. ISIS territorial defeat July 2017. International
support for reconstruction. Embassies expanding operations. Full integration
maintained. Some tensions with Kurds and neighbors.

2018.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Post-ISIS recovery. International engagement
on reconstruction. Some US-Iran tensions affect Iraq. Embassies operating
normally. Full diplomatic status.

2019.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. October 2019 protests. Some tensions over
Iran influence. Embassies operating. International community engaged.
Normal diplomatic status with minor tensions.

2020.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. COVID-19 pandemic. January 2020: Soleimani
killing in Baghdad strains relations temporarily. Embassies operating.
International engagement maintained despite pandemic and regional tensions.

2021.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Political deadlock. Embassies operating. International
community engaged. Normal diplomatic integration. Some militia attacks
on embassies but relations maintained.

2022.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Political crisis throughout year. Government
formed October. Embassies operating. International engagement normal.
Iraq balances US and Iran relations. Full diplomatic status.

2023.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations. Sudani government. Active regional diplomacy.
Iraq mediates between neighbors. Embassies operating. International engagement
robust. Normal diplomatic integration.

2024.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Gaza/Israel conflict creates regional tensions.
Iraq balances relations with all parties. Embassies operating. International
engagement maintained. Normal diplomatic status.

2025.assessment:
Current year. Full diplomatic integration likely continues. Iraq maintains
balancing act between US, Iran, regional powers. Normal diplomatic status
expected to continue.

1988.assessment:
Post Iran-Iraq War. State-dominated economy. Minimal FDI due to war debt,
political instability, and Ba'athist economic policy. Foreign investment
essentially non-existent.

1989.assessment:
Pre-Kuwait invasion. Minimal FDI. State-dominated economy. Foreign investors
wary due to debt burden and political risk. Essentially closed to foreign
direct investment.

1990.assessment:
Kuwait invasion August 1990. UN sanctions imposed. FDI becomes impossible.
Economic isolation. Negative environment for any foreign investment.

1991.assessment:
Gulf War. Comprehensive UN sanctions. Zero FDI. Complete economic isolation.
No foreign direct investment possible under sanctions regime.

1992.assessment:
Comprehensive sanctions. Zero FDI. Economic isolation complete. No legal
foreign investment possible. Sanctions prevent capital inflows.

1993.assessment:
Sanctions continue. Zero FDI. Complete isolation from international capital
markets. No foreign direct investment.

1994.assessment:
Comprehensive sanctions. Zero FDI. No foreign investment possible. Iraq
isolated from global capital.

1995.assessment:
Sanctions period. Zero FDI. Oil-for-Food not yet implemented. No foreign
direct investment. Complete capital isolation.

1996.assessment:
Oil-for-Food begins but no FDI. Sanctions prevent foreign direct investment.
Oil-for-Food is trade not investment. Zero FDI.

1997.assessment:
Sanctions continue. Zero FDI. Oil-for-Food operational but no investment
inflows. Foreign direct investment prohibited by sanctions.

1998.assessment:
Sanctions period. Zero FDI. Desert Fox bombing. No foreign investment.
Iraq closed to international capital.

1999.assessment:
Late sanctions period. Zero FDI. Sanctions prevent foreign direct investment
despite some sanctions erosion on trade. Capital flows blocked.

2000.assessment:
Late Saddam era. Zero FDI. Sanctions prevent foreign investment. Oil-for-Food
corruption but no formal FDI. Iraq closed to foreign capital.

2001.assessment:
Pre-invasion. Sanctions continue. Zero FDI. No foreign direct investment
possible under sanctions regime. Iraq isolated from global capital.

2002.assessment:
Pre-invasion year. Sanctions in place. Zero FDI. Uncertainty about conflict
prevents any investment even if sanctions lifted. No foreign capital inflows.

2003.assessment:
Invasion March 2003. Regime change. Sanctions lifted May. Minimal FDI due
to war, occupation, looting. Security prevents investment. Essentially
zero FDI despite sanctions removal.

2004.assessment:
CPA period. Insurgency intensifies. Minimal FDI despite reconstruction
spending. Security prevents private investment. Foreign capital wary.
Oil sector contracts some activity.

2005.assessment:
Post-CPA. Iraqi government. Elections. FDI remains very low. Security
prevents investment. Oil sector some activity. Telecoms sector interest.
Very low FDI given security situation.

2006.assessment:
Civil war begins. FDI collapses. Security crisis prevents investment.
Minimal foreign capital inflows. Oil sector only significant activity.
Very low FDI.

2007.assessment:
Peak violence. Surge year. FDI minimal. Security prevents investment.
Oil sector some activity. Very low foreign direct investment. War deters
capital inflows.

2008.assessment:
Post-surge. Security improving. FDI increases slightly. Oil sector investment
growing. Telecoms. Still low but improving. Foreign investors beginning
to return.

2009.assessment:
Oil sector investment continues. FDI low but stable. Global financial
crisis impacts inflows. Security still constrains investment. Oil and
telecoms main sectors.

2010.assessment:
Low FDI. Oil sector investment. Political deadlock delays reforms. Security
improving but investment climate weak. Corruption, bureaucracy deter FDI.

2011.assessment:
US withdrawal December 2011. FDI low. Arab Spring regional uncertainty.
Oil sector investment continues but FDI remains low. Corruption, weak
institutions deter investment.

2012.assessment:
Low FDI. Oil production increasing attracts some investment. Non-oil FDI
minimal. Corruption, security concerns, weak institutions constrain
investment. Low inflows.

2013.assessment:
FDI increases moderately. Oil sector investment. Some non-oil sectors.
ISIS emerging creates uncertainty. Moderate FDI by Iraq standards but
still low globally.

2014.assessment:
ISIS crisis June 2014. FDI collapses in second half. Year average shows
moderate but crisis destroys investment climate. Oil prices collapse.
Security crisis. FDI flows stop.

2015.assessment:
ISIS war. FDI declines. Oil price collapse. Security crisis. Foreign
investment collapses. Only essential oil sector activity. Low FDI.

2016.assessment:
Mosul offensive. ISIS war continues. FDI low. Oil sector some activity.
Security crisis prevents investment. Low foreign capital inflows.

2017.assessment:
ISIS territorial defeat July 2017. FDI low. Post-war reconstruction needs
but investment slow to return. Security concerns persist. Low inflows.

2018.assessment:
Post-ISIS. FDI increases moderately. Reconstruction. Oil sector. Some
non-oil investment. Still low but improving. Corruption and institutions
constrain investment.

2019.assessment:
October 2019 protests. FDI declines. Political crisis. Oil sector continues
but non-oil investment deterred. Protests over corruption, unemployment.
Low FDI.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 pandemic. Oil price collapse. FDI collapses. Soleimani killing
January. Political crisis. Economic crisis. Foreign investment minimal.
Very low inflows.

2021.assessment:
Oil prices recovering. FDI remains low. Political deadlock. Corruption,
weak institutions. Oil sector some activity. Low foreign investment.

2022.assessment:
Oil boom from Russia-Ukraine war. FDI low despite high revenues. Political
crisis throughout year. Corruption, institutions deter investment. Oil
sector focus. Low FDI.

2023.assessment:
Sudani government. FDI low. Oil sector investment. Some infrastructure
focus. Corruption and weak institutions continue to deter investment.
Low inflows.

2024.assessment:
FDI remains low. Gaza war regional tensions. Oil sector focus. Corruption,
institutions, regional instability constrain investment. Low foreign
capital inflows expected.

2025.assessment:
Current year. FDI likely remains low. Structural constraints persist:
corruption, weak institutions, oil dependence. Foreign investment minimal
outside oil sector.

1988.assessment:
Full sovereignty. No foreign military presence. Iraqi military independent.
No foreign bases or combat operations. Post Iran-Iraq War period.

1989.assessment:
Full sovereignty. No foreign military presence. Iraqi armed forces independent.
No foreign combat operations or bases in Iraq.

1990.assessment:
August 1990: Kuwait invasion. US/coalition forces deploy to Saudi Arabia
(Operation Desert Shield) but not in Iraq. No foreign forces in Iraqi
territory. Iraq maintains sovereignty despite crisis.

1991.assessment:
January-February 1991: Gulf War (Operation Desert Storm). Massive US-led
coalition combat operations. Air campaign, ground invasion. Brief occupation
of southern Iraq then withdrawal. Active foreign combat operations throughout
year. Coalition forces withdraw by year end.

1992.assessment:
No-fly zones established by US/UK/France over northern and southern Iraq.
Continuous foreign air presence and enforcement operations. Periodic strikes.
No ground forces but airspace sovereignty violated. Contested presence.

1993.assessment:
No-fly zones continue. US cruise missile strikes January 1993. Continuous
foreign air presence. Iraqi sovereignty contested but no ground forces.
Periodic military strikes.

1994.assessment:
No-fly zones enforced. Continuous US/UK/France air presence. Iraq challenges
no-fly zones periodically. Contested airspace sovereignty. No ground forces.

1995.assessment:
No-fly zones continue. Foreign air presence. Iraq periodically contests
enforcement. Airspace sovereignty violated but no ground forces present.

1996.assessment:
No-fly zones enforced. September 1996: US cruise missile strikes (Operation
Desert Strike) after Iraq incursion into Kurdish areas. Continuous foreign
air presence. Contested sovereignty.

1997.assessment:
No-fly zones continue. US/UK air patrols. Iraq challenges enforcement
periodically. Contested airspace. No ground forces but continuous foreign
air presence.

1998.assessment:
No-fly zones enforced. December 1998: Operation Desert Fox - 4-day US/UK
bombing campaign after weapons inspectors leave. Active foreign combat
operations during December. No-fly zones resume after strikes.

1999.assessment:
No-fly zones continue. Frequent US/UK strikes against Iraqi air defense
sites. Iraq challenges no-fly zones regularly. Contested airspace. Low-intensity
aerial conflict.

2000.assessment:
No-fly zones enforced. Periodic US/UK strikes. Iraq fires on coalition
aircraft regularly. France withdraws from no-fly zone enforcement. Contested
airspace continues.

2001.assessment:
No-fly zones enforced by US/UK. Regular strikes against air defense sites.
Post-9/11 increases tensions. Iraq contests foreign presence. Airspace
sovereignty violated.

2002.assessment:
No-fly zones continue. US military buildup in Kuwait and region for potential
invasion. Weapons inspections (UNMOVIC). Contested airspace. Military
buildup for invasion but not yet in Iraq.

2003.assessment:
March 2003: US-led invasion. Massive uninvited foreign combat operations.
Occupation begins April. 150,000+ US troops. Coalition Provisional Authority.
Iraq under military occupation. Major uninvited foreign presence throughout
year.

2004.assessment:
US-led occupation continues. June 2004: Sovereignty transfer to Iraqi
Interim Government but 140,000+ US troops remain in combat operations.
Invited post-sovereignty but massive presence. Active combat operations
against insurgency.

2005.assessment:
US-led forces continue combat operations. 140,000+ US troops plus coalition.
Invited by Iraqi government but massive presence. Active combat against
insurgency. Sovereignty formal but limited due to foreign military dominance.

2006.assessment:
Civil war begins. US forces 140,000+ in active combat. Coalition forces
invited by Iraqi government but massive presence dominates security landscape.
Active foreign combat operations throughout year.

2007.assessment:
US surge begins. Troop levels increase to 170,000. Peak US military presence.
Active foreign combat operations. Invited forces but dominant military
presence. Coalition forces in major combat role.

2008.assessment:
Post-surge. US troop levels decline to ~140,000 by year end. Active combat
operations continue but violence declining. Invited forces. Status of
Forces Agreement signed November 2008 for withdrawal.

2009.assessment:
US troop withdrawal begins. June 2009: US forces withdraw from cities.
Troop levels ~130,000. Active combat operations transitioning to Iraqi
forces. Invited significant forces but combat role declining.

2010.assessment:
US troop drawdown continues. August 2010: End of combat operations declared,
transition to "advise and assist." Troop levels decline to ~50,000. Significant
invited forces in support/training role.

2011.assessment:
December 2011: Complete US military withdrawal. Coalition forces depart.
Small residual presence (embassy guards, trainers ~200). Transition from
significant invited forces to minimal presence. Iraq regains military
sovereignty.

2012.assessment:
Post-US withdrawal. Minimal foreign military presence (embassy security,
small training mission ~200). Iraq fully sovereign. No foreign combat
forces. Limited invited advisors only.

2013.assessment:
Minimal foreign military presence. Limited advisors and embassy security.
Iraq maintains military sovereignty. ISIS emerging but no foreign combat
forces yet. Limited invited advisors only.

2014.assessment:
June 2014: ISIS crisis. August 2014: US begins airstrikes. US advisors
deploy (~3,000 by year end). Coalition forms. Active foreign combat operations
(airstrikes) resume. Invited forces in combat support role.

sources.citation: US returns to Iraq August 2014 anti-ISIS

2015.assessment:
Anti-ISIS coalition operations. ~3,500 US troops plus coalition forces.
Active airstrikes, training, advising. Invited forces. Active foreign
combat operations supporting Iraqi forces. Coalition combat operations.

2016.assessment:
Mosul liberation campaign. US troop levels increase to ~5,000. Coalition
forces active. Airstrikes, special operations, advisors. Active foreign
combat operations. Invited but significant combat role.

2017.assessment:
Mosul liberation July 2017. ISIS territorial defeat. US ~5,000 troops.
Coalition forces continue operations. Active combat operations throughout
year. Invited forces in combat support role.

2018.assessment:
Post-ISIS. Coalition forces ~5,000 US troops plus coalition partners.
Transition from active combat to training/advising. Some operations continue.
Significant invited forces. Parliament votes for foreign troop withdrawal
(not binding).

2019.assessment:
Coalition forces ~5,000 US troops. Training and advising mission. Limited
counter-ISIS operations. October protests create anti-foreign sentiment.
Significant invited forces but contested domestically.

2020.assessment:
January 2020: Soleimani killing in Baghdad. Parliament votes (non-binding)
for US withdrawal. US ~5,000 troops. Coalition drawdown begins. Some bases
transferred to Iraqis. Significant invited forces but politically contested.

sources.citation: Soleimani killing aftermath; Iraq parliament vote

2021.assessment:
US troop drawdown. Combat mission officially ends December 2021. Transition
to ~2,500 troops in advise/train role. Limited invited advisors. No official
combat mission but troops remain.

2022.assessment:
US ~2,500 troops in training/advising role. Coalition forces minimal.
Limited invited advisors. Occasional counter-ISIS operations. Militia
attacks on US bases. Limited foreign military presence.

2023.assessment:
US ~2,500 troops. Limited training/advising mission. Coalition presence
minimal. Invited but limited advisors. Occasional counter-ISIS operations.
Militia attacks continue. Limited foreign military presence.

2024.assessment:
US ~2,500 troops. Gaza war increases regional tensions and militia attacks.
US-Iraq agree to transition coalition mission. Limited invited advisors.
Plans for further drawdown but presence continues.

sources.citation: US forces Iraq Gaza war period

2025.assessment:
Current year. US forces likely ~2,500. Limited training/advising mission
expected to continue. Coalition transition ongoing. Limited invited advisors
probable status.

1988.assessment:
Post Iran-Iraq War. Minimal new displacement. War returnees. Stable minimal
displacement situation. No major refugee outflows.

1989.assessment:
Pre-Kuwait invasion. Minimal displacement. Stable population. No major
refugee flows. Some Kurdish displacement from Anfal campaign aftermath.

1990.assessment:
August 1990: Kuwait invasion. Iraqis expelled from Kuwait. Hundreds of
thousands return from Kuwait and Gulf states. Some displacement from
Kuwait back to Iraq. Moderate displacement.

1991.assessment:
Gulf War. March 1991: Kurdish uprising crushed. Mass Kurdish exodus to
Turkey and Iran (~2 million). Southern marsh Arabs displaced. Massive
displacement crisis. Catastrophic outflows. Safe havens established.

1992.assessment:
Post-Gulf War. Many Kurds return to safe haven. Marsh Arabs continue
displacement. Sanctions era emigration begins. Significant outflows as
economic crisis deepens. Returns and outflows.

1993.assessment:
Sanctions era. Marsh Arab draining campaign. Displacement continues.
Economic refugees flee to Jordan, Syria, Iran. Moderate to significant
outflows. Hyperinflation drives emigration.

1994.assessment:
Continued sanctions emigration. Marsh Arabs displacement ongoing. Iraqis
flee to Jordan, Syria primarily. Moderate outflows. Brain drain. Economic
crisis drives emigration.

1995.assessment:
Sanctions emigration continues. Iraqis in Jordan, Syria, Iran. Moderate
outflows. Economic distress. Some political refugees. Steady emigration.

1996.assessment:
Oil-for-Food begins but emigration continues. Intra-Kurdish conflict
creates some displacement. Moderate outflows. Economic and political
refugees.

1997.assessment:
Sanctions era emigration continues. Iraqis in neighboring countries.
Oil-for-Food provides some relief but emigration ongoing. Moderate outflows.

1998.assessment:
Desert Fox bombing December creates some displacement. Sanctions emigration
continues. Moderate outflows to Jordan, Syria. Economic refugees.

1999.assessment:
Late sanctions period. Emigration continues. Iraqi diaspora growing in
Jordan, Syria, Iran, West. Moderate outflows. Economic and political
refugees.

2000.assessment:
Late Saddam era. Continued emigration to Jordan, Syria. Moderate outflows.
Economic situation improved slightly with Oil-for-Food but emigration
continues.

2001.assessment:
Pre-invasion. Emigration continues. Post-9/11 uncertainty. Moderate
outflows to neighbors. Iraqi diaspora established in Jordan, Syria.

2002.assessment:
Pre-invasion uncertainty. Some anticipatory emigration. Moderate outflows.
Iraqis leave anticipating conflict. Jordan and Syria destinations.

2003.assessment:
March 2003: US invasion. Significant displacement. Iraqis flee to Syria,
Jordan during invasion. Post-invasion insecurity drives emigration.
Significant outflows. ~400,000 displaced.

2004.assessment:
Insurgency intensifies. Significant outflows to Syria, Jordan. ~500,000
flee. Security deterioration drives emigration. Professionals, minorities
targeted. Significant displacement.

2005.assessment:
Sectarian tensions rising. Significant outflows. Iraqis flee to Syria,
Jordan. Internal displacement beginning. ~600-700,000 displaced. Security
deteriorating. Significant outflows.

2006.assessment:
Civil war begins. Mass displacement. ~1.5 million flee to Syria, Jordan.
Sectarian cleansing. Massive internal displacement. Mass displacement
crisis. ~5% of population displaced in year.

2007.assessment:
Peak violence. Mass displacement continues. ~2 million external refugees
total (cumulative). 2+ million internally displaced. Worst year of
displacement. Mass displacement. ~6% of population displaced in year.

2008.assessment:
Post-surge. Violence declining. New displacement slows. Some returns
begin. Significant outflows but declining from peak. Total displaced
population stable at ~4 million but new flows declining.

2009.assessment:
Security improving. New displacement minimal. Returns increasing. Moderate
outflows. Displacement crisis stabilizing. Many displaced remain but
new flows small.

2010.assessment:
Stable situation. Minimal new displacement. Some returns. Total displaced
population ~4 million but stable. Net returns possible. Stable minimal
new displacement.

2011.assessment:
US withdrawal. Stable displacement situation. Minimal new flows. Syrian
civil war begins creating reverse flows (Syrian refugees to Iraq). Stable
minimal new displacement.

2012.assessment:
Stable situation. Minimal new Iraqi displacement. Syria refugees fleeing
to Iraq. Net returns likely. Stable minimal displacement. Iraqi refugee
situation improving.

2013.assessment:
Security deteriorating. ISIS emerging. New displacement beginning in
Anbar. Syrian refugees continue arriving. Moderate new displacement.
Situation worsening.

2014.assessment:
June 2014: ISIS seizes Mosul. Catastrophic displacement. ~3 million
internally displaced by year end. Mosul, Anbar, Ninewa populations flee.
Yazidi genocide. Catastrophic displacement crisis. >10% of population
displaced.

2015.assessment:
ISIS war continues. Mass displacement ongoing. Total displaced reaches
~4.5 million. New displacement ~5%. Ongoing mass displacement as ISIS
areas contested. Humanitarian crisis.

2016.assessment:
Mosul offensive begins October. Mass displacement. Mosul battle creates
massive displacement. Total displaced ~5 million. Mass displacement from
Mosul areas. Camps overwhelmed.

2017.assessment:
Mosul liberated July 2017. Mass displacement during battle. ~1 million
displaced from Mosul. Total displaced ~6 million peak. ISIS territorial
defeat. Returns begin in some areas. Mass displacement.

2018.assessment:
Post-ISIS. Returns exceed new displacement. ~4 million returns from
displacement. Net returns. Liberated areas see population return. Still
~2 million displaced but returns substantial.

2019.assessment:
Returns continue. Net returns. ~1.5 million still displaced but returns
ongoing. Some areas difficult to return (Sinjar, Mosul destruction).
October protests create minimal new displacement. Net returns overall.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 slows returns. ~1.4 million still displaced. Net returns continue
but slower. Pandemic impacts return process. Minimal new displacement.
Net returns.

sources.citation: UNHCR Iraq COVID-19 impact on displacement 2020

2021.assessment:
Returns continue slowly. ~1.2 million displaced. Net returns. Many unable
to return due to destruction, security, documentation. Protracted displacement
for remaining IDPs.

2022.assessment:
Returns slow. ~1.1 million still displaced. Many in protracted displacement.
Net returns minimal. Camps closing but many unable to return. Net returns
but slowing.

2023.assessment:
~1 million still displaced. Returns very slow. Protracted displacement
situation. Many unable to return. Net returns minimal. Displacement
situation stable but unresolved for many.

2024.assessment:
Displacement situation stable. ~1 million protracted IDPs. Minimal new
displacement. Gaza war regional tensions create uncertainty but minimal
impact on Iraq displacement. Stable situation.

2025.assessment:
Current year. Likely stable displacement situation. ~1 million protracted
IDPs expected. Minimal new displacement. Unresolved protracted displacement
for ISIS-era IDPs. Stable minimal new displacement expected.

1988.assessment:
Post Iran-Iraq War. No comprehensive international sanctions. Iraq maintained
normal trade relations with most countries. Some US restrictions on dual-use
technology but no broad sanctions regime.

1989.assessment:
No major sanctions. Iraq trading normally with international community.
Some US export controls on sensitive technology but no economic sanctions.
Pre-Kuwait invasion period.

1990.assessment:
August 2, 1990: Iraq invades Kuwait. August 6: UN Security Council
Resolution 661 imposes comprehensive economic sanctions - total trade
embargo, asset freeze, oil export ban. Transition from no sanctions to
total isolation within days.

1991.assessment:
Full comprehensive UN sanctions. Resolution 687 (April 1991) maintains
sanctions pending weapons inspections and reparations. Complete trade
embargo except minimal humanitarian exceptions. Asset freeze. Oil exports
banned. Total economic isolation.

1992.assessment:
Comprehensive UN sanctions in full effect. No legal oil exports. Trade
prohibited except minimal humanitarian goods. UN Sanctions Committee
oversees strict enforcement. No sanctions relief. Complete economic embargo.

sources.citation: UN Iraq Sanctions Committee annual reports

1993.assessment:
Comprehensive sanctions continue. Resolution 986 (April 1995) authorizing
Oil-for-Food not yet passed. Total embargo maintained. Humanitarian
exception minimal. Complete economic isolation continues.

1994.assessment:
Comprehensive sanctions. UN debates humanitarian impact. Iraq refuses
initial Oil-for-Food proposals. Sanctions remain comprehensive with minimal
humanitarian exceptions. Complete trade embargo continues.

1995.assessment:
April 1995: Resolution 986 passed establishing Oil-for-Food program but
Iraq rejects terms. Comprehensive sanctions continue. No oil exports. Total
embargo maintained. Smuggling increases but sanctions formally comprehensive.

1996.assessment:
May 1996: Iraq accepts Oil-for-Food. December 1996: First oil exports under
program. Sanctions formally comprehensive but Oil-for-Food creates humanitarian
exception. Still broad economic sanctions with strictly controlled trade.

1997.assessment:
Oil-for-Food operational. Broad economic sanctions with humanitarian
exceptions. Oil exports allowed only through UN program. All trade controlled
by UN Sanctions Committee. Comprehensive sanctions with significant humanitarian
carve-out.

1998.assessment:
Broad sanctions with Oil-for-Food exceptions. December 1998: Operation
Desert Fox (US/UK bombing) after UNSCOM weapons inspectors leave. Sanctions
maintained but enforcement eroding. Smuggling increases. Formally comprehensive
but weakening.

1999.assessment:
Broad sanctions with humanitarian exceptions. Oil-for-Food ceiling removed.
Sanctions enforcement weakening as countries open illegal trade. Russia,
France, China opposing sanctions continuation. Still formally comprehensive
but eroding.

2000.assessment:
Broad sanctions with large humanitarian exceptions. Sanctions regime
significantly eroded. Many countries open direct trade despite UN prohibition.
Iraqi government manipulates Oil-for-Food. Still formally comprehensive
but enforcement weak.

2001.assessment:
Broad sanctions with humanitarian exceptions. May 2001: US proposes "smart
sanctions" focusing on military items. Sanctions weakening but still formally
in place. Oil-for-Food continues with increasing corruption.

2002.assessment:
Broad sanctions with humanitarian exceptions. November 2002: Resolution
1441 on weapons inspections. Sanctions maintained as pressure for disarmament.
Oil-for-Food continues. Pre-invasion uncertainty about sanctions future.

2003.assessment:
March 2003: US invasion. May 22, 2003: UN Resolution 1483 lifts sanctions
imposed in 1990. Oil-for-Food program ends November. Transition from sanctions
to post-war reconstruction. Comprehensive sanctions lifted.

2004.assessment:
No UN sanctions. CPA occupation continues. Iraq under US-led coalition
control. No international economic sanctions. Full access to international
trade and finance restored.

2005.assessment:
No sanctions. Iraqi transitional government. Full participation in international
economy. No economic restrictions. Iraq rejoins international financial
institutions. Normal trade relations.

2006.assessment:
No sanctions. Civil war but no international sanctions. Iraq maintains
normal international trade relations. Full access to global financial
system despite internal conflict.

2007.assessment:
No sanctions. Peak violence year but no international economic restrictions.
Iraq participates fully in international economy. Normal trade and financial
relations maintained.

2008.assessment:
No sanctions. Iraq full member of international community. Normal trade
relations. No economic restrictions. Post-surge stabilization. International
economic integration complete.

2009.assessment:
No sanctions. Iraq fully integrated into international economy. Normal
trade and financial relations. No restrictions. Member of international
institutions.

2010.assessment:
No sanctions. Political deadlock domestically but no international restrictions.
Iraq maintains normal international trade and financial relations. Full
global economic integration.

2011.assessment:
No sanctions. US withdrawal December 2011. Iraq sovereign and no international
sanctions. Normal trade relations. Full participation in international
economy. No restrictions.

2012.assessment:
No sanctions. Iraq fully integrated internationally. Normal trade and
financial relations. No restrictions. Member of international economic
institutions. Some individual terror designations but no country-level sanctions.

2013.assessment:
No country-level sanctions. ISIS emerging but no international sanctions
against Iraq. Normal trade relations maintained. Full international economic
access. Some terrorist designations of individuals.

2014.assessment:
No sanctions against Iraq. ISIS crisis June 2014 but international community
supports Iraqi government. UN sanctions against ISIS individuals/entities
not Iraq. Iraq maintains normal international trade relations despite crisis.

sources.citation: UN sanctions on ISIS not Iraq

2015.assessment:
No sanctions. Anti-ISIS war. International support for Iraq. UN/US sanctions
target ISIS not Iraqi government. Iraq maintains normal trade relations
with international community. No restrictions.

2016.assessment:
No sanctions. Mosul liberation campaign. International coalition supports
Iraqi government. No economic restrictions. Normal trade and financial
relations maintained. Sanctions target ISIS not Iraq.

2017.assessment:
No sanctions. ISIS territorial defeat July 2017. Iraq maintains normal
international relations. No economic restrictions. Full participation in
international trade and finance. International support continues.

2018.assessment:
No sanctions. Post-ISIS recovery. Iraq maintains normal international
economic relations. No restrictions. Full access to trade and finance.
Some US tensions over Iran ties but no sanctions.

2019.assessment:
No country-level sanctions. October 2019 protests. Iraq maintains normal
international relations. US pressure over Iran ties but no broad sanctions.
Individual sanctions on some Iraqi officials for protest violence.

2020.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions only. COVID-19 pandemic. US sanctions some
Iraqi officials and militia leaders over Iran ties and protest violence.
No broad economic sanctions. Normal trade relations maintained. Individual
designations not country-level.

2021.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions. US maintains individual designations on
militia leaders and some officials. No country-level sanctions. Iraq normal
international trade status. Individual sanctions for terrorism, corruption,
human rights.

2022.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions. US/EU maintain individual designations on
officials, militia leaders for corruption, terrorism, human rights. No
country-level economic sanctions. Iraq maintains normal international trade
relations.

2023.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions. US/EU sanction individuals for corruption,
militia activities, human rights violations. No country-level sanctions.
Iraq fully integrated in international economy. Normal trade and financial
relations.

2024.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions. US maintains designations on militia leaders,
corrupt officials. Gaza/Israel conflict creates regional tensions but no
new Iraq sanctions. Normal international trade status maintained.

2025.assessment:
Current year. Likely continuation of targeted individual sanctions on
militia leaders and corrupt officials. No indication of country-level
sanctions. Iraq maintains normal international economic status.

sources.citation: Current US sanctions policy toward Iraq

1988.assessment:
Saddam Hussein totalitarian state; no civil liberties. Political parties
banned except Ba'ath. Freedoms of speech, assembly, association, and press
non-existent. Anfal campaign against Kurds (chemical weapons). Arbitrary
arrest, torture, and execution routine. FH CL=7 (worst).

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 1988.
Human Rights Watch. Genocide in Iraq: The Anfal Campaign. 1993.

1989.assessment:
Post-Anfal Saddam regime. Complete absence of civil liberties. State security
(Mukhabarat, Amn, SSIO) monitors all aspects of life. No free press, assembly,
or association. Arbitrary detention and torture systemic.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 1989.
Amnesty International. Iraq: Systematic Torture. Annual Report 1990.

1990.assessment:
Kuwait invasion year. Civil liberties remain completely absent. Military
mobilization further restricts movement and expression. No political
opposition tolerated.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 1990.

1991.assessment:
Gulf War aftermath; Shia and Kurdish uprisings brutally suppressed. Brutal
crackdown on any dissent. Mass graves from uprising suppression. Freedom
of movement restricted by no-fly zones and internal checkpoints. Complete
absence of civil liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 1991.
Human Rights Watch. Endless Torment: The 1991 Uprising in Iraq. 1992.

1992.assessment:
Sanctions imposed; Kurdistan de facto autonomous under KRG (better civil
liberties than south/center). Central/southern Iraq: complete absence of
civil liberties. Regime repression intensifies against Shia. Marsh Arab
displacement campaigns.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 1992.
UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/1993/45.

1993.assessment:
Complete absence of civil liberties in central/southern Iraq. Marsh Arab
systematic displacement continuing. Press entirely state-controlled.
No freedom of assembly, association, or religion for non-Sunni groups.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 1993.
UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/1994/58.

1994.assessment:
No civil liberties. Inter-Kurdish fighting in north (PUK vs KDP) but this
is separate from Baghdad's repressive control. Regime introduces amputation
punishments for theft (October decree). Complete state control of information.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 1994.
Amnesty International. Iraq: Systematic Torture of Political Prisoners. 1994.

1995.assessment:
Complete absence of civil liberties. Hussein Kamel defection (August) triggers
intensified security crackdowns. All media state-controlled; internet
non-existent. No civil society permitted outside Ba'ath party organizations.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 1995.
UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/1996/61.

1996.assessment:
No civil liberties. Iraqi military re-enters Kurdistan supporting KDP (August).
Oil-for-Food begins (December) but no political liberalization accompanies it.
State terror apparatus fully operational.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 1996.
UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/1997/57.

1997.assessment:
Complete absence of civil liberties. Grand Ayatollah al-Sadr family
systematically targeted. State security apparatus fully functioning.
No press freedom, no civil society, no religious freedom for Shia.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 1997.

1998.assessment:
Grand Ayatollah Sadiq al-Sadr assassinated (February); Shia uprising
brutally suppressed. UNSCOM expelled. Complete absence of civil liberties.
Operation Desert Fox (December) does not affect internal repression.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 1998.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 1999: Iraq.

1999.assessment:
Continued repression following Sadr assassination. No civil liberties.
Mass executions of Shia activists. No freedom of movement, expression,
assembly, or religion for Shia and Kurds.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 1999.
UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/2000/43.

2000.assessment:
Complete absence of civil liberties. Abu Ghraib and Mahjar prison massacres.
No free press, assembly, or political activity outside Ba'ath framework.
State security omnipresent. Oil-for-Food corruption enriches elite.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2000.
Amnesty International. Iraq: Systematic Torture of Political Prisoners. 2001.

2001.assessment:
No civil liberties. Post-9/11 Saddam uses terrorism rhetoric to justify
further repression. No free media, assembly, association, or religious
freedom for Shia and Kurds.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2001.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2002: Iraq.

2002.assessment:
Pre-invasion final year of Ba'athist control. October prisoner amnesty
selective; no political liberalization. UN inspectors return but no civil
society permitted. FH CL=7.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2002.
Amnesty International. Iraq: Amnesty or Impunity. 2002.

2003.assessment:
