Regime falls (April 9). Civil liberties technically non-existent under
Ba'ath until April; immediate post-invasion period chaotic with limited
rights protections amid looting and collapse. CPA establishes some interim
framework but security vacuum enables abuses. FH CL=7 for the year.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2003.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2004: Iraq.

2004.assessment:
CPA governance; Transitional Administrative Law provides rights framework.
Sovereignty transferred June. New freedoms of press and assembly nominally
established but security situation severely constrains. Hundreds of new
newspapers; but journalists targeted. Abu Ghraib scandal. FH CL=6.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2004.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2005: Iraq.

2005.assessment:
Elections held (January, October, December). Constitution approved. Some
civil liberties formally established but security situation prevents exercise.
Militia activity, sectarian killings, and insurgent intimidation severely
limit civil liberties in practice. FH CL=6.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2005.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2006: Iraq.

2006.assessment:
Sectarian civil war peak. Samarra mosque bombing (February) triggers mass
civilian killings. Death squads operate with impunity. Freedom of movement
severely curtailed by sectarian checkpoints. Journalists, minorities, and
women face extreme threats. FH CL=6.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2006.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2007: Iraq.

2007.assessment:
Surge reduces violence second half. Mass displacement (over 4 million total
refugees/IDPs). Some marginal improvements in security but civil liberties
still severely constrained. Minorities, women, and LGBTQ+ persons at extreme
risk. FH CL=6.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2007.

2008.assessment:
Violence declining significantly. Some return of displaced persons. Press
more active. But security forces detain without trial; militias continue
targeting minorities; gay men targeted by militias. FH CL=6.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2008.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2009: Iraq.

2009.assessment:
Provincial elections held peacefully. US drawing down. Some improvement in
civil liberties as violence declines. Press more active; some civil society
operating. But Maliki government restricting political space; minorities
still threatened. FH CL=5.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2009.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2010: Iraq.

2010.assessment:
March elections competitive; Allawi wins but Maliki refuses power transfer.
8-month political stalemate. Press active but journalists face threats.
Civil society growing but under pressure. Maliki consolidating security
powers. FH CL=5.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2010.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2011: Iraq.

2011.assessment:
US troops depart. Maliki charges Deputy PM Hashimi with terrorism (December).
Arab Spring-inspired protests violently suppressed in Tahrir Square. Freedom
House downgrades Iraq from CL=5 to CL=6 reflecting Maliki's crackdown and
weaponized judiciary.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2011.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2012: Iraq.

2012.assessment:
Maliki government increasingly authoritarian. Journalists and bloggers
arrested. Torture in detention widespread. Sunni protest movement grows.
Civil society space shrinking. FH CL=6.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2012.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2013: Iraq.

2013.assessment:
Hawija crackdown on Sunni protest camp (April) kills 50+. Rising sectarian
violence. Journalists targeted; media offices raided. Civil society under
severe pressure. Sunni political leaders arrested. FH CL=6.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2013.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2014: Iraq.

2014.assessment:
ISIS conquers Mosul (June). In ISIS-controlled areas: complete elimination
of all civil liberties; systematic persecution of minorities, women, LGBTQ+.
Yazidi genocide. In government areas: significant restrictions continue
under Abadi. FH downgrades to CL=7 due to ISIS control.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2014.
UN Commission of Inquiry on Iraq. ISIS: Genocide against Yazidis. 2015.

2015.assessment:
ISIS controls ~40% of Iraq with total suppression of civil liberties.
Mass executions, sexual slavery, destruction of cultural heritage. In
government areas: Abadi more restrained than Maliki but PMF abuses documented.
FH CL=7.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2015.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2016: Iraq.

2016.assessment:
ISIS territory shrinking but still significant (Mosul, Anbar). In ISIS areas:
total suppression. Fallujah retaken (June) with PMF abuses. Mosul offensive
starts (October). In government areas: some civil liberties function but
PMF operates with impunity. FH CL=7.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2016.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2017: Iraq.

2017.assessment:
Mosul liberated (July); ISIS territorial defeat (December). Mass casualties
in Mosul battle. Post-liberation PMF abuses in Sunni areas. Kurdistan
independence referendum (September) triggers Baghdad military response.
FH CL=7 reflecting continued PMF abuses and conflict.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2017.
Amnesty International. At Any Cost: Civilian Catastrophe in Mosul. 2017.

2018.assessment:
Post-ISIS period; some improvement in civil liberties outside conflict zones.
Elections held (May). Basra protests (September) cracked down with live fire.
Press active but journalists threatened. Minorities in liberated areas face
ongoing discrimination. FH CL=6.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2018.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2019: Iraq.

2019.assessment:
Tishreen protest movement (October) cracked down with live fire; 500+
protesters killed; journalists targeted. Security forces and PMF use
excessive force. Civil liberties severely curtailed during protests.
Internet shut down during crackdown. FH CL=6.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2019.
Human Rights Watch. Iraq: Security Forces Kill Protesters. 2019.

2020.assessment:
Protest movement continues but reduced. Activists and journalists
assassinated by unknown armed groups. US-Iran proxy conflict restricts
civil space. COVID-19 restrictions used to suppress protests. FH CL=6.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2020.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2021: Iraq.

2021.assessment:
Continued assassinations of activists and journalists. Early elections (October).
Freedom of press constrained. PMF operates above law. Minorities still
displaced from Sinjar, Nineveh Plain. FH CL=6.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2021.
UN Assistance Mission Iraq. Annual Report 2021.

2022.assessment:
Political stalemate (10 months); Sadrist-PMF Green Zone clashes. Press
freedom constrained. Activists continue to face threats. Sudani government
formed (October) with reform promises. FH CL=6.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2022.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2023: Iraq.

2023.assessment:
Sudani government pursuing stability. LGBTQ+ persecution intensifies
(anti-transgender law passed 2023). Press freedom constrained. Some
improvement in security situation. Minorities still facing discrimination.
FH CL=6.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2023.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2024: Iraq.

2024.assessment:
Continued severe restrictions on civil liberties. US-Iran proxy conflict
flares around Gaza. Press freedom constrained. LGBTQ+ persecution continues
after 2023 law. FH CL=6.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2024.

2025.assessment:
Projection: FH CL=6 expected to be maintained. No significant liberalization
expected under current political system. PMF retains political power; civil
society constrained.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2025.

1988.assessment:
Saddam Hussein rules through absolute control; Ba'ath party elite unified
under Saddam via patronage, fear, and kinship networks. Tikrit tribal network
(Majid clan) dominates. No independent elite factions tolerated. Apparent
unified elite but based on coercion.

sources.citation: Makiya, Kanan. Republic of Fear. University of California Press, 1998.
Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2000.

1989.assessment:
Post-war consolidation under Saddam. Elite cohesion maintained through
purges of potential rivals. Ba'ath party structure intact. Saddam's sons
Uday and Qusay gaining power. Apparent unity through terror.

sources.citation: Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2000.

1990.assessment:
Elite cohesion maintained during Kuwait invasion. Military and political
elite unified under Saddam's decision-making. No dissent permitted or
visible. Invasion decision taken without significant elite opposition.

sources.citation: Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2000.

1991.assessment:
Gulf War defeat and uprisings test elite cohesion; no significant elite
defection occurs. Military commanders purged after defeat. Security
elite remains loyal to Saddam; civilian losses from war do not produce
elite fragmentation.

sources.citation: Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2000.

1992.assessment:
Sanctions and defeat create strains but Saddam maintains elite control.
Security services ensure elite loyalty through surveillance and fear.
Internal purges continue. No organized elite opposition.

sources.citation: Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2000.

1993.assessment:
Elite cohesion maintained under sanctions pressure. Saddam increasingly
relies on tribal networks and Tikriti kinship groups. Some elite resentment
of economic deterioration but no organized opposition within ruling circle.

sources.citation: Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2000.

1994.assessment:
Elite cohesion strained by economic collapse but maintained. Saddam
expands tribal patronage to manage elite; distributes assets from
sanctions-era corruption. Competition within security services contained
by Saddam.

sources.citation: Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2000.

1995.assessment:
Hussein Kamel defection (August) - Saddam's son-in-law and head of WMD
programs - reveals elite fractures. Kamel's defection to Jordan and return
(execution on return February 1996) shows limits of family loyalty. More
significant fracture than any previous event.

sources.citation: Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2000.

1996.assessment:
Kamel executed (February); elite cohesion reasserted through demonstrative
punishment. Iraq intervenes in Kurdistan (August) demonstrating military
loyalty. Oil-for-Food begins (December) distributes resources for elite
management. Cohesion restored but fragile.

sources.citation: Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2000.

1997.assessment:
Elite cohesion maintained through Oil-for-Food corruption patronage. Saddam
manages elite through economic benefits from sanctions-busting. No significant
defections. Uday-Qusay rivalry for succession beginning to surface.

sources.citation: Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2000.

1998.assessment:
UNSCOM expelled; limited international pressure. Elite cohesion maintained.
Uday Hussein increasingly influential; tensions between Uday and Qusay for
succession position becoming visible. Saddam manages tension.

sources.citation: Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2000.

1999.assessment:
Elite cohesion maintained; Saddam increasingly relies on Qusay for security
apparatus. Uday's erratic behavior limits his succession viability. No
organized elite opposition; security services prevent coordination.

sources.citation: Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2000.

2000.assessment:
Elite cohesion managed through patronage, fear, and Oil-for-Food corruption.
Uday-Qusay competition for succession managed but not resolved. No elite
defections. Regime stable but structurally brittle.

sources.citation: Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2000.

2001.assessment:
Post-9/11 US pressure increases. Elite cohesion maintained but Saddam
aware of potential threats. Loyalty enforced through security monitoring.
No significant defections; elite believes Saddam will weather US pressure
as he did in 1991.

sources.citation: Woods, Kevin et al. The Mother of All Battles. USNI Press, 2008.

2002.assessment:
Pre-invasion preparations; Saddam restructures military command. Elite
cohesion maintained but some military officers privately expecting defeat.
Saddam's inner circle maintains outward loyalty. No major defections despite
US military buildup.

sources.citation: Woods, Kevin et al. The Mother of All Battles. USNI Press, 2008.
Gordon, Michael & Trainor, Bernard. Cobra II. Pantheon, 2006.

2003.assessment:
Regime collapse (April). Ba'athist elite fragments completely upon US
invasion. Most military units collapse without fighting; senior officials
flee or surrender. Qusay and Uday killed (July). Saddam captured (December).
Complete elite fragmentation as old regime dissolved.

sources.citation: Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. 3rd ed. Cambridge University Press, 2007.
Gordon, Michael & Trainor, Bernard. Cobra II. Pantheon, 2006.

2004.assessment:
New elite forming from multiple competing groups: former Ba'athists,
returning exiles (Chalabi, Allawi, Hakim), Sadrists, Kurdish parties
(KDP, PUK). CPA-appointed Governing Council represents extreme elite
fragmentation with competing sectarian and ethnic factions.

sources.citation: Diamond, Larry. Squandered Victory. Times Books, 2005.
Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. 3rd ed. Cambridge University Press, 2007.

2005.assessment:
Elections produce elite defined by sectarian-ethnic lines: SCIRI/Badr, Dawa,
Sadrists (Shia); KDP/PUK (Kurdish); Sunni factions fragmented and partly
boycotting. Maliki emerges as compromise Shia leader. Deep elite divisions
between major blocs.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Iraq After the Iraqi Elections. 2005.

2006.assessment:
Maliki government formed (May) after 5-month stalemate. Sectarian civil war
peak. Elite fragmentation severe: Shia factions (Dawa, SCIRI/ISCI, Sadrists)
compete; Sunni factions divided between armed resistance and political
engagement; Kurdish elite maintaining separate power. Complete fragmentation.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. The Next Iraqi War? 2006.

2007.assessment:
Surge and Awakening create some Sunni elite realignment toward government.
Sadr suspends militia operations (August). But elite fragmentation continues:
Maliki consolidating at expense of rivals; inter-Shia competition intense.
Open factional conflict within government itself.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Iraq's Civil War. 2007.

2008.assessment:
Maliki increasingly consolidating power. Basra operation (March) demonstrates
Iraqi state asserting against Sadrists. SOFA negotiations. Provincial
elections planned. Elite competition remains intense but Maliki gaining
dominance. Open factional conflict.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Iraq's New Battlefront. 2008.

2009.assessment:
Provincial elections (January) restructure elite balance; Maliki's State of
Law wins significantly. SOFA takes effect; US drawdown. Maliki increasingly
dominant but faces opposition from Sadrists, ISCI, and Kurdish parties.
Significant competition managed but not resolved.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Iraq and the Kurds: Confronting Withdrawal Fears. 2009.

2010.assessment:
March elections: Allawi's Iraqiya wins more seats but Maliki refuses to
relinquish power. 8-month government formation stalemate demonstrates
elite inability to manage competition peacefully. Iranian and US interference
in coalition formation. Active elite power struggles.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Iraq's Uncertain Future. 2010.

2011.assessment:
Maliki consolidates authoritarian control; charges Hashimi (Sunni VP) with
terrorism; weaponizes judiciary against rivals. Sadrists return to government.
US withdrawal removes external arbiter. Elite competition increasingly
conducted through state coercion.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Failing Oversight: Iraq's Unchecked Government. 2011.

2012.assessment:
Maliki government faces no-confidence motion (May, fails). Sunni bloc
increasingly alienated. Kurdish-Maliki tensions over Kirkuk and oil.
Inter-Shia competition managed. Elite power struggles through institutional
manipulation rather than accommodation.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Iraq and the Kurds. 2012.

2013.assessment:
Maliki targeting Sunni leaders; Hawija massacre; rising ISIS threat. Elite
consensus forming that Maliki's sectarianism is counterproductive but no
unified alternative. Competition among Shia factions; Sunni elite split
between government cooperation and armed resistance support.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Make or Break: Iraq's Sunnis and the State. 2013.

2014.assessment:
ISIS crisis forces Maliki replacement by Abadi (August). Elite temporarily
unifies against ISIS threat. PMF formation as new elite actor. Kurdish
forces (Peshmerga) operate independently. Abadi more consensual than Maliki.
Managed competition during crisis period.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Raising the Stakes in Iraq. 2014.

2015.assessment:
Anti-corruption protests and Abadi reform agenda. PMF as major new elite
actor competing with formal military. Kurdish political elite increasingly
asserting autonomy. Shia factions maintain anti-ISIS cooperation. Managed
competition among major blocs.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Reform or Illusion? 2015.

2016.assessment:
Mosul offensive begins (October). Protest movement demands reform. Elite
competition between PMF-aligned factions and formal state actors. Shia
factions increasingly competitive in anticipation of post-ISIS political
settlement. Kurdish independence ambitions rising.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Arming Iraq's Kurds. 2016.

2017.assessment:
ISIS territorial defeat (December) triggers intense elite competition over
post-ISIS resources and power. Kurdistan independence referendum (September)
opposed by Baghdad and Iran; Baghdad military response to Kirkuk drives
Kurdistan out of Kirkuk. Open factional conflict in post-ISIS political
settlement.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Oil, Power, and Conflict. 2017.

2018.assessment:
Elections (May) produce fragmented parliament; 5 months to form government.
Abadi replaced by Abdul-Mahdi (October). Intense Shia factional competition
between Sadrists, Fatah (PMF), and technocratic blocs. Sunni and Kurdish
elites marginalized. Significant factional conflict.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Iraq after IS. 2018.

2019.assessment:
Tishreen protest movement challenges entire elite. October protests demand
systemic change; elite threatened by cross-sectarian popular uprising.
Abdul-Mahdi resigns (November). Elite competition over who controls
caretaker government. Open fractional conflict over protest response.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Tishreen: Iraq's Protest Movement. 2019.

2020.assessment:
US-Iran conflict plays out in Iraq (Soleimani killing, January). 7-month
caretaker government; Kadhimi forms government (May) as compromise between
factions. PMF-Iran axis vs. US-aligned factions; Sadrists semi-independent.
Elite competition managed but tensions high.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Iraq: Evading the Gathering Storm. 2020.

2021.assessment:
October elections; Sadrists win plurality (73 seats); Coordination Framework
(pro-Iran) forms alternative bloc. 7-month elite stalemate over government
formation. Significant competition not resolved. Sadr-PMF tensions rising.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Iraq's Ticking Clock. 2021.

2022.assessment:
10-month stalemate; Sadrists resign parliament seats (August); Coordination
Framework gains control; Green Zone clashes (August-September); Sudani
compromise government formed (October). Sadr retires from politics.
Managed competition after crisis.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Iraq's Political Imbroglio. 2022.

2023.assessment:
Sudani government pursuing stabilization; managing competition between
Coordination Framework factions; Kurdistan-Baghdad oil dispute partially
resolved. Elite competition managed through patronage distribution.
More stable than 2019-2022 period.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group. Iraq Under Sudani. 2023.

2024.assessment:
Continued management of competing elite factions under Sudani. PMF retains
political power; US-Iran tensions through Iraqi proxy flares around Gaza
conflict. Kurdistan-Baghdad oil framework developing. Competition managed
but structural divisions unresolved.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2024. Country report Iraq.

2025.assessment:
Projection: Continued managed competition under Sudani. No resolution of
structural elite divisions between Shia factions, Sunni blocs, and Kurdish
parties. PMF retains significant autonomous power. Managed but fragmented.

1988.assessment:
Ba'athist authoritarian state with functional but entirely regime-controlled
institutions. Legislature (National Assembly) rubber-stamps decisions; courts
serve regime; bureaucracy operational but subordinate to party structure.
Institutions function to serve dictatorship, not public accountability.

sources.citation: Makiya, Kanan. Republic of Fear. University of California Press, 1998.
Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2000.

1989.assessment:
Post-war consolidation. Ba'athist institutions intact; party apparatus fully
controls state functions. Bureaucracy operational. No meaningful legislative
independence. Courts serve security services. Institutions deliver services
but lack accountability.

sources.citation: Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2000.

1990.assessment:
Kuwait invasion (August) shifts state focus to military mobilization.
Institutions continue to function under emergency wartime conditions.
Ba'ath party apparatus intact; bureaucracy operational.

sources.citation: Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2000.

1991.assessment:
Gulf War followed by Shia and Kurdish uprisings nearly collapses state in
south and north. Regime survives but institutions severely damaged. Kurdish
area effectively outside central government control after uprising. Southern
Shia areas under military occupation rather than normal governance.

sources.citation: Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2000.
Human Rights Watch. Endless Torment: The 1991 Uprising in Iraq. 1992.

1992.assessment:
Regime reconsolidates in central Iraq but loses Kurdistan to autonomous
Kurdish administration (KRG established). Southern Iraq under heavy security
presence replacing normal governance. Central institutions functioning for
regime survival under sanctions.

sources.citation: Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2000.
UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/1993/45.

1993.assessment:
Institutions functioning under severe sanctions constraints. Oil-for-Food
not yet established; sanctions devastate public services. State organs
focused on regime survival. Courts, ministries, military all functioning
but severely resource-constrained.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/1994/58.

1994.assessment:
Sanctions continue devastating public services. Hyperinflation destroys
civil servant salaries; brain drain from professional class. Institutions
nominally functioning but capacity severely degraded by economic crisis.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/1995/56.

1995.assessment:
Continued institutional degradation under sanctions. Public health, education,
and utilities increasingly non-functional. Hussein Kamel defection (August)
reveals WMD programs and causes internal purges affecting institutional
coherence.

sources.citation: Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2000.

1996.assessment:
Oil-for-Food program begins (December), providing some resources. Institutions
still operating under severe constraints. Central authority maintained through
security services rather than normal governance structures. Kurdistan remains
outside central control.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/1997/57.

1997.assessment:
Oil-for-Food provides limited resources for institutional functions. Security
apparatus remains primary governing mechanism. Public health and education
partially restored through humanitarian imports. No political reforms.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/1998/67.

1998.assessment:
UNSCOM inspections expulsion (December) isolates Iraq further. Institutions
functional for regime survival; military and security services prioritized.
Public services constrained but somewhat maintained through Oil-for-Food.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/1999/37.

1999.assessment:
State institutions functioning primarily to maintain regime control. Oil-for-Food
corruption diverts funds. Public services severely degraded from 1990 levels
despite oil revenues. Kurdistan's KRG institutions developing separately.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/2000/43.

2000.assessment:
Institutions operating in sanctions environment. Ba'ath party controls all
state organs. Public services partially maintained. Security apparatus
dominant. Extensive Oil-for-Food corruption documented later. Kurdistan KRG
increasingly functional separately.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/2001/42.

2001.assessment:
Post-9/11 tensions increase; institutions mobilized for potential conflict.
Ba'ath party apparatus intact. Security services dominant. Public services
constrained. No meaningful institutional reform.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/2002/44.

2002.assessment:
Pre-invasion year; regime prepares for potential US attack. Institutions
mobilized for war preparation. UN inspectors return (November). State
apparatus fully focused on regime survival rather than governance.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/2003/42.

2003.assessment:
US-led invasion (March-April) destroys Ba'athist state. Regime collapse
(April 9). De-Ba'athification and disbanding of Iraqi Army (May CPA Orders
1 and 2) eliminate state institutions. Ministry looting. Complete institutional
collapse in all sectors; Coalition Provisional Authority (CPA) as emergency
governance.

sources.citation: Bremer, L. Paul. My Year in Iraq. Simon & Schuster, 2006.
Diamond, Larry. Squandered Victory. Times Books, 2005.
RAND Corporation. Occupying Iraq: A History of the Coalition Provisional Authority. 2009.

2004.assessment:
CPA attempts institution-building; Iraqi Governing Council (IGC) established.
Transitional Administrative Law (TAL) signed (March). Sovereignty transferred
(June 28). Iraqi Interim Government formed. New institutions embryonic and
highly contested. Security forces being rebuilt from scratch under US guidance.

sources.citation: Tripp, Charles. A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press, 2007 (3rd edition).
Diamond, Larry. Squandered Victory. Times Books, 2005.

2005.assessment:
Elections (January, October, December) create formal institutional structures.
Constitution approved (October). Council of Representatives elected
(December). Institutions nominally forming but operating in extreme insecurity.
Army and police rebuilding. Courts highly constrained.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2005. Country report Iraq.

2006.assessment:
Maliki government formed (May). Peak civil war disrupts all institutions.
Sectarian death squads operate within security forces. Judiciary functioning
minimally. Legislature meets but cannot control security situation.
Institutions severely compromised by sectarian infiltration.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2006. Country report Iraq.
International Crisis Group. The Next Iraqi War? Sectarianism and Civil Conflict. 2006.

2007.assessment:
Surge operations begin; Anbar Awakening. Institutions functioning partially
but still severely constrained by violence. Legislature dysfunctional
(unable to pass key legislation). Courts slowly developing. Security forces
improving but infiltrated. Violence declining second half.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2007. Country report Iraq.

2008.assessment:
Basra Operation and Sadr City battles demonstrate improved Iraqi Security
Forces capacity. Violence declining significantly. SOFA negotiated (November).
Provincial elections planned. Institutions rebuilding slowly. Legislature more
functional but Maliki increasingly concentrating power.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2008. Country report Iraq.

2009.assessment:
Provincial elections (January) held successfully. US combat forces drawing
down. Iraqi Security Forces assuming more responsibility. Legislature and
ministries more operational. Corruption endemic but institutions present.
Maliki consolidates power around security services.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2009. Country report Iraq.

2010.assessment:
March elections produce Allawi coalition majority; Maliki refuses to cede
power; 8-month government formation stalemate. Democratic norms undermined.
Institutions functioning but dominated by Maliki's informal power networks.
US withdrawal accelerating.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2010. Country report Iraq.
International Crisis Group. Iraq's New Political Landscape. 2010.

2011.assessment:
US military withdrawal completed (December). Maliki consolidates authoritarian
control; Deputy PM Hashimi charged with terrorism (December). Institutions
functioning but increasingly subordinated to Maliki's informal rule.
Judiciary weaponized against political opponents.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2011. Country report Iraq.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2012: Iraq.

2012.assessment:
Rising Sunni protests against Maliki government; judiciary increasingly
politicized; ISA (Iraqi Security Architecture) concentrated in PM office;
institutions formally present but executive dominance growing. Violence
rising.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2012. Country report Iraq.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2013: Iraq.

2013.assessment:
Sunni protest camp crackdowns (Hawija massacre). ISF and PMF abuses increasing.
Legislature passing few laws; budget deadlocks. Judiciary politically
compromised. Maliki running for third term. Institutions seriously degraded
by sectarianism and executive domination.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2013. Country report Iraq.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2014: Iraq.

2014.assessment:
ISIS conquers Mosul (June) and one-third of Iraq; ISF collapses in north.
Maliki replaced by Abadi (August). Institutions partially collapse in
ISIS-controlled areas. PMF formation operates outside formal state structures.
Institutions severely degraded in conflict zones.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2014. Country report Iraq.
International Crisis Group. Falluja's Faustian Bargain. 2014.

2015.assessment:
ISIS controls Mosul, Fallujah, large areas. Government institutions absent
in ISIS territories. Abadi government fighting war while attempting reforms.
PMF laws passed formalizing militias. Institutions deeply compromised
by ongoing conflict and corruption.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2015. Country report Iraq.

2016.assessment:
Mosul offensive begins (October). Anti-corruption protests (July). Abadi
reform agenda partly stalled by political opposition. Institutions functional
in government-held areas but severely constrained. Fallujah retaken (June).
PMF Law passed October.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2016. Country report Iraq.
Human Rights Watch. World Report 2017: Iraq.

2017.assessment:
Mosul liberated (July). ISIS territorial defeat (December). Iraq reclaims
Kirkuk from Kurdistan (October) after independence referendum. Post-ISIS
reconstruction begins. Institutions reasserting authority in liberated areas.
PMF deeply embedded in security structure.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2017. Country report Iraq.
International Crisis Group. Iraq After the Fall of IS: The Sectarian State. 2017.

2018.assessment:
Elections (May) produce fragmented parliament; Abadi replaced by Abdul-Mahdi
(October). Institutions rebuilding post-ISIS. Basra protests (September) over
services highlight institutional failures. PMF integrated into formal security
structures but maintaining independent command.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2018. Country report Iraq.

2019.assessment:
Tishreen protest movement (October) demands fundamental institutional reform;
security forces kill 500+ protesters; PM Abdul-Mahdi resigns (November).
Institutions functioning but widely perceived as captured by elites and
foreign interests. Crisis of legitimacy.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2019. Country report Iraq.
Human Rights Watch. Iraq: Security Forces Kill Protesters. 2019.

2020.assessment:
US killing of Soleimani (January) and retaliatory Iranian strikes on US bases.
Kadhimi forms government (May) after 7-month caretaker. Institutions formally
operating but legitimacy low. Ongoing protests. COVID-19 strains already-weak
health institutions. Early elections announced.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2020. Country report Iraq.
International Crisis Group. Iraq's Tishreen Uprising. 2020.

2021.assessment:
Early elections (October) held with very low turnout (~41%); Sadr bloc wins
but rejects coalition; 7-month stalemate. US combat mission formally ends
(July). Institutions functioning but political stalemate undermines
policymaking capacity.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2021. Country report Iraq.
UN Assistance Mission Iraq. Annual Report 2021.

2022.assessment:
10-month political stalemate; Sadrist withdrawal (August) enables non-Sadrist
coalition; Green Zone clashes; Sudani forms government (October). New
government has reformist mandate but faces entrenched system. Institutions
broadly functional in formal sense.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2022. Country report Iraq.
International Crisis Group. Iraq's Political Imbroglio. 2022.

2023.assessment:
Sudani government pursuing anti-corruption measures with limited success.
Institutions more stable than 2019-2022 period. PMF still semi-autonomous.
Kurdistan-Baghdad disputes over oil revenues. Legislature passing budgets.
Broadly functional but deeply corrupted system.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2023. Country report Iraq.

2024.assessment:
Continued political stability under Sudani. Budget passed. Kurdistan-Baghdad
oil dispute partially resolved. PMF integration into formal structures
advancing. US-Iran proxy tensions flare around Gaza conflict. Institutions
functioning with endemic corruption.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2024. Country report Iraq.

2025.assessment:
Projection: Continued functioning but corrupted institutions under Sudani
government. PMF retains significant autonomous power. No major institutional
transformation expected. Broadly partially functional with reform rhetoric
exceeding reality.

1988.assessment:
During the final year of the Iran-Iraq War (1980-1988), Iraq maintained one of the largest
military establishments in the Middle East. Defense spending was consuming an estimated
30-40% of GDP during the war years, though official reliable data is unavailable from SIPRI
for this period. The war ended in August 1988 with a UN-brokered ceasefire, but the military
apparatus remained intact and Iraq was heavily indebted.

sources.citation: SIPRI Military Expenditure Database (does not cover Iraq pre-2004 comprehensively)

1989.assessment:
Post-Iran-Iraq War period. Iraq was demobilizing but maintaining a large military force.
The country was deeply indebted from the war and military spending remained very high.
Saddam Hussein was consolidating domestic control and preparing for regional assertiveness.

1990.assessment:
Iraq invaded Kuwait on August 2, 1990, triggering UN sanctions from August 6. Military
spending was extremely high relative to GDP given the Kuwait invasion mobilization.
UN sanctions immediately halted oil exports, collapsing the economy while the military
remained fully deployed. Data is unavailable for this period.

1991.assessment:
Gulf War (January-February 1991) and subsequent ceasefire. Iraq's military was
devastated by coalition airpower but Saddam Hussein retained power. GDP collapsed
dramatically under sanctions making any percent-of-GDP measure misleading. Formal
military spending data is not available from SIPRI for this period.

2002.assessment:
Pre-invasion period; US-UK buildup toward Iraq. Saddam Hussein's regime maintained
a large military apparatus despite sanctions-era resource constraints. No SIPRI data
is available for this period.

2003.assessment:
US-led invasion March 2003 dissolved the Iraqi military. The CPA (Coalition Provisional
Authority) disbanded the Iraqi army in May 2003. Iraqi government military spending
was effectively zero for most of the year as the regime collapsed and new institutions
were not yet formed. SIPRI data is not available.

sources.citation: Coalition Provisional Authority Order 2 (Dissolution of Iraqi Army, May 2003)

2004.assessment:
First full year of post-Baathist Iraq. The new Iraqi security forces were being built
from scratch following the dissolution of the army in 2003. Formal military expenditure
was low at 1.7% of GDP as the new army was nascent, though US coalition forces were
bearing most security costs. The insurgency was escalating rapidly.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS (sourced from SIPRI), accessed 2026-03-02

2005.assessment:
Iraqi military spending growing as new security forces expanded. First Iraqi
elections held January 2005. Sectarian insurgency intensifying. Official
spending of 2.2% of GDP represents Iraqi government forces only; total security
cost vastly higher when including coalition forces.

2011.assessment:
US forces withdrew from Iraq by end of 2011 per Status of Forces Agreement. Iraqi
government took full responsibility for security spending. Military expenditure at
2.3% of GDP reflects the government's growing security apparatus as US withdrawal
approached.

2014.assessment:
ISIS captured Mosul in June 2014 and declared a caliphate. The Iraqi army suffered
major defeats and the government declared a state of emergency. Despite the security
crisis, official military expenditure as a percent of GDP was 2.95%, reflecting
pre-crisis budgets and the sudden fall in oil revenues that shrank GDP.

2015.assessment:
Military spending surged to 5.4% of GDP as Iraq fought to retake territory from ISIS.
This is the highest recorded level in the post-2003 period, reflecting emergency
military mobilization including Popular Mobilization Forces (PMF/Hashd al-Shaabi)
alongside the regular military. Oil price collapse simultaneously reduced GDP.

2017.assessment:
Mosul was retaken from ISIS in July 2017 after a nine-month battle. Military
expenditure remained elevated at 3.9% of GDP reflecting ongoing operations.
The defeat of ISIS territorial control marked the end of the most intense
conventional military phase of the conflict.

1996.assessment: Iraq under Saddam Hussein in 1996 had a PV estimate of -1.82, reflecting extreme political instability and violence. The post-Gulf War period saw continued Kurdish insurgency in the north, Shia suppression in the south, and Ba'athist security forces maintaining order through terror. Despite surface-level Ba'athist control, the underlying conditions for political violence were deeply embedded.

1998.assessment: By 1998, with no-fly zones partitioning northern and southern Iraq, the country remained highly unstable. Operation Desert Fox (December 1998) demonstrated continued external military threat. The Ba'athist regime maintained control through repression of Shia and Kurdish populations, while the ongoing effects of sanctions deepened societal stress.

2000.assessment: Iraq in 2000 scored -1.73, among the most politically unstable states globally. The regime faced continued international isolation and periodic US/UK airstrikes under no-fly zones. Internal repression of Shia and Kurdish populations, sporadic regime-organized attacks on dissidents abroad, and rising post-Gulf War social tensions characterized this period.

2002.assessment: With the US publicly building a case for invasion in 2002, Iraq's political instability score of -1.60 reflected growing external threats combined with internal repression. Post-9/11 dynamics elevated perceptions of Iraq as a destabilizing force in the region, while the regime continued its suppression of internal dissent.

2003.assessment: The US-led invasion in March 2003 and subsequent state collapse drove Iraq's political stability score to -2.39 — among the very worst globally. The collapse of Ba'athist order, looting, insurgency emergence, and CPA's inability to provide security created conditions of near-total political instability in the post-invasion period.

2004.assessment: Iraq in 2004 scored -3.18 — the lowest ever recorded in this dataset and the very bottom of the global distribution at the 0th percentile. The insurgency was in full escalation with IED attacks, Coalition casualty peaks, Abu Ghraib, and Fallujah battles. Political instability was at its absolute nadir in the post-invasion chaos.

2005.assessment: Despite three rounds of elections in 2005 (January, October constitutional referendum, December parliamentary), Iraq remained in extreme political violence at -2.69. Al-Qaeda in Iraq attacks on Shia communities and escalating insurgency operations made 2005 one of the deadliest years of the conflict.

2006.assessment: The 2006 Samarra mosque bombing triggered full-scale sectarian civil war, driving Iraq to -2.83 at the 0th percentile globally. Shia-Sunni mass displacement, daily mass casualty bombings, and death squads operating within security forces made 2006 the peak of sectarian violence. UN and US government assessments described Iraq as on the verge of state failure.

2007.assessment: During the 2007 US troop surge, Iraq scored -2.77. While the surge ultimately reduced sectarian violence by end-2007, the annual average remained catastrophic. The Anbar Awakening began turning Sunni tribes against AQI, marking the beginning of a slow, fragile stability improvement that would take years to register in WGI scores.

2008.assessment: A meaningful improvement to -2.47 in 2008 reflected the declining sectarian violence following the surge and the Operation Charge of the Knights against Sadr's militia in Basra. The SOFA negotiation with the US indicated increasing sovereign capacity, but Iraq remained in the extreme instability range at the 1.92nd percentile.

2009.assessment: Further improvement to -2.17 in 2009 as US combat forces withdrew from Iraqi cities in June under SOFA terms. Major bombings against government ministries in August demonstrated continued extreme vulnerability. Despite progress, Iraq remained firmly in the extreme instability range, bottom 2.37th percentile globally.

2010.assessment: The prolonged 2010 government formation crisis (9 months to form a government after March elections) coincided with a slight worsening to -2.24. Continued Al-Qaeda in Iraq bombings and the political deadlock undermined stability gains. The score remained in the extreme instability range despite overall violence declining from 2006-2007 peaks.

2011.assessment: The US military withdrawal from Iraq in December 2011 coincided with an improvement to -1.85. While significant, this still places Iraq at the 4.27th percentile globally. The Syrian civil war beginning in 2011 created new regional instability that would eventually spill into Iraq via returning jihadists and cross-border flows.

2012.assessment: Iraq's stability worsened to -1.93 in 2012 as Maliki's exclusionary Sunni policies drove new political crises and violence resumed. Mass protests in Sunni provinces against perceived sectarian discrimination contributed to an environment fertile for ISIS reconstitution. The Syrian conflict overflow was beginning to be felt along border areas.

2013.assessment: Violence escalated sharply in 2013 to -2.01, with ISIL/ISIS reconstituting in western Iraq and bombing campaigns returning to levels unseen since 2008. Fallujah saw combat between Iraqi forces and ISIL in late 2013. Maliki's repression of the Hawija protest encampment in April 2013 was a key turning point accelerating Sunni alienation.

2014.assessment: ISIS captured Mosul in June 2014 and declared a caliphate, controlling a third of Iraqi territory. The score plummeted to -2.48, among the worst globally. The collapse of Iraqi army divisions, Yazidi genocide, and mass displacement of millions marked one of the most severe political stability crises since the 2003 invasion itself.

2015.assessment: Despite ongoing ISIS control of major cities, the score improved slightly to -2.25 in 2015 as Iraqi forces and PMF began counteroffensives with US air support. The ISIS threat remained existential but counterterrorism operations showed early successes. Iraq remained at the 2.86th percentile globally — firmly in extreme instability.

2016.assessment: The score of -2.30 in 2016 during the Mosul offensive reflects continued extreme instability despite territorial gains against ISIS. The October 2016 Mosul offensive launched with coalition support, while ISIS retaliatory bombings in Baghdad caused hundreds of civilian casualties. PMF sectarian conduct during recapture operations added new destabilization dimensions.

2017.assessment: The liberation of Mosul in July 2017 and the formal declaration of ISIS territorial defeat did not yet improve the score (-2.30), as post-ISIS stabilization remained fragile. The Kurdish independence referendum in September 2017 triggered a constitutional crisis and Iraqi military operation against Kirkuk, adding new destabilizing dimensions.

2018.assessment: A surprising deterioration to -2.52 in 2018 despite ISIS territorial defeat reflects the continued fragility of Iraqi stability. Post-ISIS insurgency, ongoing PMF violence, renewed US-Iran proxy conflict on Iraqi soil, and a prolonged government formation crisis after May elections all contributed. Basra protests in September 2018 turned violent with dozens killed.

2019.assessment: Iraq's worst post-2010 score of -2.61 in 2019 reflects the October 2019 Tishreen uprising where security forces and pro-Iranian militias killed over 700 protesters. US-Iran tensions escalated sharply with strikes on PMF forces. The assassination of Soleimani and al-Muhandis in January 2020 (attributed to this period's dynamics) represented existential sovereignty challenges.

2020.assessment: Iraq's score improved marginally to -2.47 in 2020 as Kadhimi's government attempted to balance US-Iran tensions on Iraqi soil. COVID-19 provided a pretext for limiting protests, reducing visible political violence temporarily. ISIS continued low-level insurgency in rural areas. Rocket attacks on US facilities and assassinations of activists continued.

2021.assessment: The 2021 score of -2.38 reflects an attempted assassination of PM Kadhimi via drone attack by pro-Iranian militias in November and continued ISIS insurgency operations. The October 2021 elections produced a protracted government formation crisis. Iraq remained in the bottom 2nd percentile globally for political stability.

2022.assessment: The August 2022 Sadr movement clashes in Baghdad's Green Zone killed 30 people amid government formation disputes. Despite the Sudani government finally forming in October 2022, the score of -2.41 reflects the intense political volatility of the crisis period. Iraq remained deeply unstable with ISIS insurgency, militia violence, and ongoing political confrontation.

2023.assessment: Iraq's score held at -2.41 in 2023 despite the Sudani government's relative political stability compared to the formation crisis period. Continued ISIS insurgency in rural areas, Iranian-backed militia rocket attacks on US forces (escalating after October 7), and regional spillovers from the Gaza conflict kept political stability at extreme instability levels.

1988.assessment:
Final year of Iran-Iraq War with heavy casualties from ongoing conventional
warfare, chemical weapons use, and War of the Cities missile attacks on
civilian centers. Anfal campaign against Kurds ongoing.

sources.citation: Cordesman & Wagner. The Lessons of Modern War Vol. II: Iran-Iraq War. 1990.

1989.assessment:
Post-war period with state repression against political opponents, Kurdish
populations, and suspected dissidents; low-intensity internal security
operations.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch. Iraq: Nowhere to Go. 1990.

1990.assessment:
Kuwait invasion (August) resulted in limited immediate violence; domestic
repression continued against dissidents and ethnic minorities; pre-war
military mobilization.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch. Iraq: Nowhere to Go. 1990.

1991.assessment:
Gulf War (January-February) combat deaths, followed by catastrophic violence
during Shia and Kurdish uprisings (March) and brutal regime suppression.
Includes combatants and civilians.

1994.assessment:
Continued regime repression; inter-Kurdish fighting in northern Iraq
(PUK-KDP); state executions of political prisoners; limited insurgent
activity.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/1995/56.

1995.assessment:
Escalated inter-Kurdish warfare (PUK vs KDP); regime repression in
central/southern Iraq; defections and internal purges; Hussein Kamel
defection (August) triggers security crackdown.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/1996/61.

1996.assessment:
Iraqi military intervention in Kurdistan (August-September) supporting KDP
against PUK; regime executes opposition members; US Operation Desert Strike

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/1997/57.

1997.assessment:
Reduced Kurdish fighting after Washington Agreement mediation; continued
regime repression targeting Shia clerics and dissidents; political
executions; Grand Ayatollah al-Sadr family targeted.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/1998/67.

1998.assessment:
Operation Desert Fox airstrikes (December); assassination of Grand Ayatollah
al-Sadr II triggers Shia unrest and brutal crackdown; continued political
repression and executions.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/1999/37.

1999.assessment:
Continued suppression of Shia populations; Grand Ayatollah Sadiq al-Sadr
supporters targeted; political executions; no major conflict events; no-fly
zone enforcement.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/2000/43.

2000.assessment:
Prison massacres reported; political executions; targeting of alleged
dissidents; low-level resistance activity; Oil-for-Food program corruption
consolidates regime control.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/2001/42.
Amnesty International. Iraq: Systematic Torture of Political Prisoners. 2001.

2001.assessment:
Continued political repression; executions; minimal armed resistance; regime
focuses on internal security; post-9/11 regional tensions; no major conflict.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/2002/44.

2002.assessment:
Pre-invasion year; political executions including mass prisoner release (Oct)
followed by selective re-arrests and killings; regime consolidation; no armed
conflict; UN inspections return.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Iraq. E/CN.4/2003/42.
Amnesty International. Iraq: Amnesty or Impunity. 2002.

2003.assessment:
US-led invasion (March-May) caused majority of deaths; post-invasion looting
and lawlessness; emerging insurgency in summer/fall; sectarian targeting
begins; regime collapse violence.

sources.citation: Conetta, Carl. The Wages of War: Iraqi Combatant and Noncombatant Fatalities. 2003.
Brookings Institution. Iraq Index. 2004 quarterly reports.

2006.assessment:
Civil war peak following Samarra mosque bombing (Feb); mass sectarian
killings; ethnic cleansing campaigns; death squads operate openly; Baghdad
security deteriorates catastrophically; tens of thousands displaced.

sources.citation: UN Assistance Mission Iraq. Casualty Reports 2006.
UN Assistance Mission Iraq. Monthly Reports 2007.

2008.assessment:
Violence declining significantly due to Surge and Awakening. Battle of
Basra (March-May) against Mahdi Army. Sadr City offensive. IBC documented
~9,000-10,000 civilian deaths - still high but half of 2007 levels.

2009.assessment:
Continued decline as US combat troops reduce operations. Major bombings
continue targeting government ministries (August). Seasonal upsurge around
elections. IBC documented ~5,000 civilian deaths. Still high but trending
down.

2010.assessment:
Post-election political stalemate (8 months to form government). Violence
rises around March elections; continued insurgent attacks. AQI weakened but
still operational. IBC documented ~4,000 civilian deaths.

2011.assessment:
US military withdrawal completed (December). Violence slight uptick around
withdrawal. Maliki government increasingly sectarian; Sunni protests begin
late 2011. AQI continues attacks. IBC documented ~3,500-4,000 civilian
deaths.

2012.assessment:
Violence rising after US withdrawal. AQI reconstitutes as ISI (Islamic State
of Iraq). Sunni protests intensify against Maliki government. Sectarian
tension increasing. IBC documented ~4,600 civilian deaths - rising from
previous years.

sources.citation: UN Assistance Mission Iraq. Annual Report 2012.

2013.assessment:
Sharp increase in violence. ISI (ISIS precursor) expands; Sunni protest
crackdowns (Hawija massacre April); sectarian bombings dramatically increase;
Maliki forcibly disperses Sunni protest camps. IBC: ~9,000 civilian deaths.
Violence reaching levels not seen since 2008.

sources.citation: UN Assistance Mission Iraq. Annual Report 2013.

2014.assessment:
ISIS conquers Mosul (June), Tikrit, and one-third of Iraq. Sinjar massacre
of Yazidis. Formation of Popular Mobilization Forces (PMF). ISIS territorial
control over ~40% of Iraq. IBC documented ~17,000+ civilian deaths; total
including combatants likely ~20,000.

sources.citation: UN Assistance Mission Iraq. Annual Report 2014.

2015.assessment:
Continued ISIS territorial control; Iraqi Security Forces and PMF begin
counter-offensive; Tikrit retaken (April); Ramadi falls to ISIS (May),
retaken (December). ISIS suicide bombings in Baghdad. Intense combat in
Anbar province. IBC documented ~14,000-17,000 civilian deaths.

sources.citation: UN Assistance Mission Iraq. Annual Report 2015.

2016.assessment:
ISIS territory shrinking but still controls Mosul. Fallujah retaken (June).
Mosul offensive begins (October). PMF abuses documented in liberated areas.
Baghdad bombings continue (Karada July 2016 kills ~300). High civilian
casualties from air and ground combat.

sources.citation: UN Assistance Mission Iraq. Annual Report 2016.

2017.assessment:
Battle of Mosul (November 2016-July 2017) produces catastrophic civilian
casualties; ISIS declared defeated (December 2017); massive casualties in
urban warfare; PMF operations in Sunni areas produce displacement. IBC
documented ~13,000+ civilian deaths in Mosul battle alone; total ~25,000.

sources.citation: UN Assistance Mission Iraq. Annual Report 2017.
Amnesty International. At Any Cost: The Civilian Catastrophe in West Mosul, Iraq. 2017.

2018.assessment:
Post-ISIS territorial defeat but continued insurgent attacks; ISIS low-level
guerrilla campaign; PMF consolidation of political power; protests in Basra
(September) over services. Sharp decline from 2017 peak. IBC ~3,000-4,000
civilian deaths.

2019.assessment:
Continued ISIS insurgent attacks; Tishreen protest movement (October)
response includes security force killings of ~500+ protesters; US-Iran
proxy tension rises. Violence broadly declining but protest crackdown
adds significant toll.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch. Iraq: Security Forces Kill Protesters. 2019.

2020.assessment:
US killing of General Soleimani (January) and retaliatory Iranian missile
strikes on US bases in Iraq; protests continue but reduced; ISIS insurgency
continues in rural areas; COVID-19 reduces public gatherings. Continued
US-Iran proxy conflict on Iraqi soil.

2022.assessment:
Post-election political crisis (10 months without government); PMF and Sadr
movement clashes (August-September) kill ~30 in Green Zone; al-Kadhimi
assassination attempt (November); ISIS low-level insurgency continues.
IBC ~1,500-2,000 civilian deaths.

sources.citation: UN Assistance Mission Iraq. Annual Report 2022.

2023.assessment:
Continued ISIS low-level insurgency in Kirkuk, Diyala, Saladin; PMF retains
significant military and political power; US-Iran proxy attacks continue;
Sudani government seeks balance. Violence at lowest level since 2003 but
still significant. IBC ~1,200-1,500 deaths.

sources.citation: UN Assistance Mission Iraq. Annual Report 2023.

2024.assessment:
US-Iran tensions elevated after October 7 Gaza conflict; PMF attacks on US
bases in Iraq/Syria; US retaliatory strikes on PMF targets in Iraq; ISIS
attacks continue in rural areas. Violence remains at historically lower
post-2023 levels.

2025.assessment:
Relatively stable low-level conflict. ISIS insurgency at reduced capacity.
US forces drawing down further. PMF political integration ongoing.
Projection based on trajectory.

1988.assessment:
Iran-Iraq War final year. Saddam Hussein's government maintained full control
over Iraqi territory except for contested areas in the south near Iranian border.
War ended August 1988. Baathist regime consolidated authority.

1989.assessment:
Post-war period. Saddam regime in full control. Harsh repression of Kurds
(Anfal campaign tail end) and Shia. No insurgencies. Totalitarian control
across all Iraqi territory.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Genocide in Iraq (1993)

1990.assessment:
August 1990: Iraq invades and occupies Kuwait. Iraqi government maintains full
control of Iraq proper. Brief period of territorial expansion (Kuwait annexation)
before international response. No internal challenges to regime authority.

1991.assessment:
Gulf War. January-February: Coalition forces liberate Kuwait. March: Shia uprising
in south, Kurdish uprising in north. Saddam brutally suppresses both by April.
~10-15% of territory briefly outside government control during uprisings. By year
end, regime reasserted control but Kurdish areas increasingly autonomous.

sources.citation: Iraq Since the Gulf War: Prospects for Democracy (1994)

1992.assessment:
No-fly zones established (Operation Northern Watch, Operation Southern Watch).
Kurdish areas north of 36th parallel increasingly autonomous under de facto
Kurdish Regional Government. Baghdad controls center/south but ~15-20% of
territory (Kurdistan) outside effective central authority.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq and the Kurds, 2003

1993.assessment:
No-fly zones continued (north of 36th parallel, south of 32nd parallel).
Kurdish Regional Government consolidated control in northern Iraq (~15-20% of
territory). Baghdad maintained firm control over Arab Iraq. Low-level Kurdish
internal conflicts but no challenge to Baghdad in areas it controlled.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq and the Kurds: The Brewing Battle over Kirkuk, 2006
Gunter, M. The Kurdish Predicament in Iraq (1999)

1994.assessment:
Kurdish civil war began (KDP vs PUK conflict in Kurdistan). No-fly zones
remained in effect. Baghdad retained control of Arab Iraq. Kurdistan (~15-20%
of territory) outside central government control but fragmented between rival
Kurdish factions. Saddam's regime unchallenged in areas it controlled.

sources.citation: Romano, D. The Kurdish Nationalist Movement (2006)
Stansfield, G. Iraqi Kurdistan: Political Development (2003)

1995.assessment:
Kurdish civil war continued. Baghdad attempted intervention in Kurdish areas
but withdrew. Kurdistan (~15-20% of territory) remained outside Baghdad control,
divided between KDP and PUK. Arab Iraq under full Baathist control despite
sanctions. Saddam's regime maintained territorial control outside Kurdish areas.

sources.citation: Stansfield, G. Iraqi Kurdistan: Political Development (2003)
ICG Report: Iraq and the Kurds, 2003

1996.assessment:
Baghdad intervened in Kurdistan at KDP invitation, capturing Erbil briefly in
August-September 1996 before withdrawing. Kurdish civil war continued. No-fly
zones expanded south to 33rd parallel. Kurdistan (~15-20% of territory) remained
outside sustained Baghdad control. Arab Iraq under full regime authority.

sources.citation: O'Leary, B. et al. The Future of Kurdistan in Iraq (2005)
Stansfield, G. Iraqi Kurdistan: Political Development (2003)

1997.assessment:
Kurdish civil war began winding down. KDP controlled Erbil/Dohuk areas, PUK
controlled Sulaymaniyah area. Kurdistan (~15-20% of territory) remained outside
Baghdad control under de facto Kurdish administration. Baghdad maintained full
control of Arab Iraq. No-fly zones enforced by US/UK.

sources.citation: Romano, D. The Kurdish Nationalist Movement (2006)
ICG Report: Iraq and the Kurds, 2003

1998.assessment:
Washington Agreement (September 1998) ended Kurdish civil war. KDP and PUK
agreed to power-sharing. Kurdistan (~15-20% of territory) solidified as autonomous
region outside Baghdad control. Operation Desert Fox (US/UK airstrikes December
1998). Baghdad maintained control over Arab Iraq despite international pressure.

sources.citation: Stansfield, G. Iraqi Kurdistan: Political Development (2003)

1999.assessment:
Post-civil war stabilization in Kurdistan. KDP administered Erbil and Dohuk
governorates, PUK administered Sulaymaniyah governorate (~15-20% of total Iraqi
territory). Both operated separate administrations outside Baghdad authority.
Baghdad maintained full control of Arab Iraq. No-fly zones continued.

sources.citation: O'Leary, B. et al. The Future of Kurdistan in Iraq (2005)
ICG Report: Iraq and the Kurds, 2003

2000.assessment:
Continued dual Kurdish administration in Kurdistan Region (~15-20% of Iraqi
territory). Baghdad maintained full control over Arab Iraq. Oil-for-Food
program operating in both Baghdad-controlled and Kurdish areas. No significant
challenges to territorial status quo. No-fly zones enforced.

sources.citation: Stansfield, G. Iraqi Kurdistan: Political Development (2003)
ICG Report: Iraq and the Kurds, 2003

2001.assessment:
Status quo maintained. Kurdistan Region (~15-20% of territory) under dual KDP/PUK
administration. Baghdad controlled Arab Iraq with no internal challenges. Post-9/11
environment increased international focus on Iraq but no change in territorial
control. No-fly zones continued enforcement.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq and the Kurds, 2003
Cordesman, A. Iraq's Military Capabilities, CSIS (2002)

2002.assessment:
Pre-invasion year. Kurdistan Region (~15-20% of territory) under KDP/PUK control
outside Baghdad authority. Baghdad maintained firm control of Arab Iraq. UN weapons
inspections resumed. Iraqi opposition groups coordinated externally but no territorial
challenges to regime. No-fly zones continued.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq Backgrounder: What Lies Beneath, October 2002
Cordesman, A. Iraq's Military Capabilities, CSIS (2002)

2003.assessment:
Following the 2003 invasion, central government controlled
Baghdad and major cities but large portions of the Sunni
triangle were contested by insurgent groups. Coalition forces
provided security in some areas but did not constitute
effective central government control.

2004.assessment:
Sunni insurgency intensified dramatically. First and Second Battles of Fallujah
(April, November 2004). Muqtada al-Sadr's Mahdi Army uprising. Anbar province,
parts of Baghdad, Mosul, and other areas heavily contested. Interim Iraqi government
formed June 2004 but had limited territorial control. Estimated ~30-40% of territory
contested or outside effective government authority.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq: Can Local Governance Save Central Government?, October 2004
Hashim, A. Insurgency and Counter-Insurgency in Iraq (2006)

2005.assessment:
Transitional government elected January 2005, permanent government December 2005.
Sunni insurgency continued with Al-Qaeda in Iraq active. Anbar province (Sunni
triangle) largely contested. Sectarian violence increasing. Government controlled
Baghdad, Shia south, Kurdish north secure but autonomous. Estimated ~25-30% of
territory contested, primarily Anbar and mixed Sunni-Shia areas.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq: Don't Rush the Constitution, June 2005
Cordesman, A. Iraq's Evolving Insurgency, CSIS (2005)

2006.assessment:
Civil war peak following Samarra mosque bombing (February 2006). Massive sectarian
violence between Sunni and Shia militias. Multiple armed groups operating:
Al-Qaeda in Iraq, Mahdi Army, Badr Corps, Sunni insurgents. Baghdad divided into
sectarian enclaves. Anbar, Diyala, parts of Baghdad contested. Estimated ~30-40%
of territory contested or controlled by non-state actors.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq's Civil War, the Sadrists and the Surge, February 2008
Cordesman, A. Iraq's Sectarian and Ethnic Violence, CSIS (2007)

2007.assessment:
US troop surge began (January 2007). Anbar Awakening: Sunni tribes turned against
Al-Qaeda in Iraq. Continued high violence but gradual security improvements in
second half of year. Anbar, Diyala, parts of Baghdad, Mosul contested. Sons of
Iraq (Sahwa) militias allied with government. Estimated ~30-40% contested but
improving by year end.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq's Civil War, the Sadrists and the Surge, February 2008
Kahl, C. et al. The Anbar Awakening (2007)

2008.assessment:
Surge effects consolidated. Charge of the Knights operation (March 2008) in Basra
against Mahdi Army. Violence declined significantly. Al-Qaeda in Iraq weakened.
Government extended control over most areas. Anbar largely pacified through Sahwa.
Some areas in Diyala, Mosul, parts of Baghdad still contested. Estimated ~20-25%
of territory with contested control, declining through year.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq After the Surge I: The New Sunni Landscape, April 2008
Cordesman, A. Iraq: Winning the Battle, Losing the War?, CSIS (2008)

2009.assessment:
Continued security improvements. US combat troops withdrew from cities (June 2009).
Iraqi Security Forces took primary security responsibility. Violence remained low
compared to 2006-2007. Residual Al-Qaeda in Iraq cells in Mosul, parts of Diyala
and Anbar. Estimated ~10-15% of territory with significant insurgent activity,
mostly rural areas.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq's New Battlefront: The Struggle over Ninewa, September 2009

2010.assessment:
Parliamentary elections (March 2010), prolonged government formation. Security
remained relatively stable. Residual Islamic State of Iraq (ISI, precursor to ISIS)
activity in rural Anbar, Ninewa, Diyala. Government maintained control over major
cities and most territory. Estimated ~10-15% with significant insurgent presence,
primarily rural/desert areas.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq's Uncertain Future: Elections and Beyond, February 2010

2011.assessment:
US combat forces completed withdrawal (December 2011). Iraqi government maintained
control of major cities and most territory. Islamic State of Iraq (ISI) weakened
but conducting attacks. Low-level insurgency in Anbar, Ninewa, Diyala. Political
tensions rising (Sunni-Shia, Arab-Kurd). Estimated ~5-10% of territory with minor
insurgent activity, mostly remote areas.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Failing Oversight: Iraq's Unchecked Government, September 2011

2012.assessment:
Post-US withdrawal. Government maintained territorial control but violence increasing.
ISI beginning resurgence, aided by Syrian civil war spillover. Sunni protests began
(December 2012) against Maliki government. Attacks rising but no territorial losses.
Estimated ~5-10% of territory with insurgent activity, primarily Anbar and Ninewa
rural areas.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq's Secular Opposition: The Rise and Decline of Al-Iraqiya, July 2012
Knights, M. & al-Tamimi, A. The Evolution of ISIS, CTC Sentinel (2014)

2013.assessment:
Violence escalated significantly. ISI rebranded as Islamic State of Iraq and Syria
(ISIS) in April 2013. Sunni protests continued. Government crackdown on protest camps.
ISIS gained strength but had not seized major territory yet. Attacks increased across
Anbar, Ninewa, Diyala, Baghdad. Estimated ~5-10% territory with significant insurgent
presence, setting stage for 2014 offensive.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Make or Break: Iraq's Sunnis and the State, August 2013
Fishman, B. The Master Plan: ISIS, al-Qaeda (2016)

2014.assessment:
Catastrophic territorial losses. ISIS seized Fallujah (January 2014), Mosul
(June 10, 2014), Tikrit, and large portions of Anbar and Ninewa governorates.
Declared caliphate June 29, 2014. Iraqi army collapsed in north. Estimated peak
ISIS control: ~35-40% of Iraqi territory including major cities (Mosul, Fallujah,
Ramadi later). Kurdish forces (Peshmerga) held Kurdish areas. Baghdad, Shia south,
and Kurdistan under government/Kurdish control.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq: Falluja's Faustian Bargain, April 2014
Knights, M. The Long Haul: Rebooting US Security Cooperation in Iraq, June 2014
Hashim, A. The Islamic State: From al-Qaeda Affiliate to Caliphate (2014)

2015.assessment:
ISIS held ~30-35% of Iraqi territory. Coalition airstrikes supporting Iraqi forces
began recapture operations. Tikrit recaptured (March-April 2015), Ramadi recaptured
(December 2015). ISIS still held Mosul, Fallujah, and large swaths of Anbar and
Ninewa. Popular Mobilization Forces (PMF/Hashd) mobilized. Slow territorial gains
by government throughout year.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq: Conflict Alert, February 2015
Knights, M. & Mello, A. The Best Thing America Built in Iraq, Politico (2015)
Heras, N. & Hokayem, E. The War Against ISIS in Iraq, CSIS (2015)

2016.assessment:
Continued anti-ISIS operations. Fallujah recaptured (June 2016). Battle for Mosul
began (October 2016), ongoing through year end. ISIS territory shrinking but still
held Mosul, western Anbar, pockets in Ninewa. Estimated ~25-35% of territory under
ISIS control at start of year, declining to ~20-25% by year end as Mosul operation
progressed.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Fight or Flight: The Desperate Plight of Iraq's Minority Communities, August 2014
Knights, M. The Future of Iraq's Armed Forces, CTC Sentinel (2016)
Robinson, L. et al. Assessment of the Politico-Military Campaign to Counter ISIL, RAND (2016)

2017.assessment:
Major turning point. Mosul fully recaptured (July 9, 2017) after 9-month battle.
Western Anbar towns recaptured. Prime Minister Abadi declared victory over ISIS
(December 9, 2017). ISIS territorial control collapsed to ~10-15% by mid-year,
less than ~5% by year end, limited to remote desert areas. Government restored
control over virtually all Iraqi territory. Residual ISIS cells remained.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq's Paramilitary Groups: The Challenge of Rebuilding a Functioning State, July 2018
Knights, M. & Mello, A. The Iraqi Security Forces: A Primer, CTC Sentinel (2017)
Robinson, L. et al. The Defeat of ISIS in Iraq, RAND (2018)

2018.assessment:
Post-ISIS territorial control restored. Government controlled all Iraqi territory.
Parliamentary elections (May 2018), prolonged government formation. ISIS reduced
to insurgent cells conducting attacks from remote areas (deserts, rural Diyala,
Kirkuk). No territorial control by ISIS. Minor insurgent activity in isolated areas.
Kurdistan Region maintained autonomous status.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq's Paramilitary Groups: The Challenge of Rebuilding a Functioning State, July 2018
Knights, M. The Salafi-Jihadi Movement in Iraq Since 2011, CTC (2018)

2019.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained. October protests (Tishreen movement) in Baghdad
and southern cities challenged government but not territorial control. ISIS continued
low-level insurgent attacks in rural areas. Kurdistan Region autonomous. Government
controlled all Iraqi territory with minor insurgent activity in remote locations.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Reversal of Fortune: Iraq's Protest Movement, November 2019
Knights, M. Squeezing ISIS in Iraq, Washington Institute (2019)

2020.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained. COVID-19 pandemic. Government transition
(Mustafa al-Kadhimi became PM May 2020). US-Iran tensions following Soleimani
assassination (January 2020). ISIS continued limited attacks in rural areas.
No territorial challenges. Government controlled all Iraqi territory.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq: Evading Economic Free Fall Amidst a Health Crisis, May 2020
Knights, M. The Evolution of ISIS Cells in Iraq, Washington Institute (2020)

2021.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained. Parliamentary elections (October 2021).
ISIS continued as insurgent cells in rural Diyala, Kirkuk, Salah al-Din conducting
occasional attacks. No territorial control by non-state actors. Government and
Kurdish Regional Government controlled all Iraqi territory. Disputes over disputed
territories but no loss of control.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq's Tishreen Uprising: From Barricades to Ballot Box, July 2021
Knights, M. The Mechanics of ISIS Attacks in Iraq (2021)

2022.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained. Political crisis and prolonged government
formation deadlock through much of year. Mohammed Shia al-Sudani became PM
(October 2022). ISIS attacks continued at low levels in remote areas. Government
maintained control of all Iraqi territory. No territorial challenges.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq: Preventing Another Dangerous Political Stalemate, May 2022
Knights, M. Containing ISIS Cells in Iraq, Washington Institute (2022)

2023.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained. Al-Sudani government focused on economic reforms
and service delivery. ISIS remained reduced to small cells conducting sporadic attacks
in rural areas. No territorial control by non-state actors. Government controlled
all Iraqi territory. Kurdistan Region maintained autonomous status within federal
framework.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iraq: Defusing Tensions Over the Kurdistan Region's Oil and Gas, June 2023

2024.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained. Al-Sudani government continued governance.
ISIS cells conducted very limited attacks in remote areas. No territorial control
by non-state actors. Government controlled all Iraqi territory. Kurdistan Region
maintained autonomous governance. Regional tensions (Israel-Gaza war spillover,
Iran-aligned militia activities) but no territorial challenges.

sources.citation: News reports on Iraq security situation (2024)

2025.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained. Government controlled all Iraqi territory.
ISIS reduced to minimal insurgent cells. Kurdistan Region autonomous within federal
framework. No significant territorial challenges to central government authority.
Provincial council elections anticipated. Regional dynamics continue to affect
security environment but not territorial control.
