2003.assessment:
Ba'athist budget: completely opaque, no public disclosure. Post-invasion:
CPA establishes some emergency financial framework. No Open Budget Survey
data; no public budget documents under Ba'ath or early CPA period.

sources.citation: RAND Corporation. Occupying Iraq: A History of the CPA. 2009.

2004.assessment:
CPA governance with some financial reporting. Development Fund for Iraq
(DFI) established with some external oversight by IAMB. Transition to
sovereign government (June). Budget processes embryonic. IBP does not
survey Iraq. Minimal transparency.

sources.citation: International Advisory and Monitoring Board for Iraq (IAMB). Annual Reports 2004.
US General Accounting Office. Rebuilding Iraq. 2004.

2005.assessment:
New government developing budget systems. Oil revenues dominant. Ministry
of Finance beginning institutional development. IBP not yet surveying Iraq.
Budget documents produced but not publicly accessible. Minimal transparency.

2006.assessment:
IBP Open Budget Survey 2006 does not include Iraq. Civil war conditions
severely limit budget transparency. Security situation prevents normal
budget implementation. Off-budget spending significant. Minimal disclosure.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership. Open Budget Survey 2006.

2007.assessment:
IBP Open Budget Survey 2008 first covers Iraq; score ~2 (effectively zero).
Budget process non-transparent; civil war limits institutional capacity.
Significant off-budget expenditure. No meaningful public access to budget
information.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership. Open Budget Survey 2008. Iraq.

2008.assessment:
IBP OBS 2008: Iraq score 2/100 (essentially no transparency). Budget
documents not publicly available; no pre-budget statements published; no
legislature or SAI scrutiny capacity. Among lowest scoring countries globally.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership. Open Budget Survey 2008. Iraq.

2009.assessment:
IBP OBS 2010 covers 2009. Iraq score improves slightly but remains very
low (~12-15). Some budget documents starting to become available but not
systematically published. Provincial elections year; some fiscal transparency
improvements needed for electoral credibility.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership. Open Budget Survey 2010. Iraq.

2010.assessment:
IBP OBS 2010: Iraq score 2/100 again. Executive Budget Proposal produced
but not published. No in-year reports or audit reports published. Citizens
budget: not produced. Legislature oversight minimal.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership. Open Budget Survey 2010. Iraq.

2011.assessment:
US withdrawal; Maliki government. Budget transparency remains minimal. No
major improvements in public disclosure practices. IBP OBS 2012 will cover
this period. Maliki government more interested in political control than
fiscal transparency.

sources.citation: World Bank. Iraq Public Finance Review. 2011.

2012.assessment:
IBP OBS 2012: Iraq score 4/100. Marginal improvement from 2/100 but
still essentially zero transparency. Executive Budget Proposal available
but not widely published. No pre-budget statement, citizens budget, or
in-year reports published.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership. Open Budget Survey 2012. Iraq.

2013.assessment:
Interpolated year between IBP surveys (2012: 4, 2015: 1). Budget transparency
declining under Maliki's increasing authoritarianism. Fiscal controls
weakening. ISIS expansion beginning to impact fiscal governance.

2014.assessment:
ISIS takeover of Mosul (June) severely disrupts fiscal system. Off-budget
military expenditure massive. Maliki replaced by Abadi. IBP OBS 2015 will
show very low score. Budget transparency further degraded by crisis.

2015.assessment:
IBP OBS 2015: Iraq score 1/100, near zero. ISIS controls significant
territory. Military emergency budget dominates. No publicly available
budget documents. Legislature unable to exercise oversight. Effectively
no budget transparency.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership. Open Budget Survey 2015. Iraq.

2016.assessment:
War costs dominate; Mosul offensive begins (October). Oil price collapse
creates fiscal crisis; IMF stand-by agreement negotiated. Some improvement
in fiscal reporting to IMF but not public. IBP 2017 survey will cover
this period.

2017.assessment:
IBP OBS 2017: Iraq score 1/100. Mosul liberated (July); ISIS defeated
(December). Still no public budget documents. Post-conflict reconstruction
funding discussions at Kuwait conference but budget transparency unchanged.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership. Open Budget Survey 2017. Iraq.

2018.assessment:
Post-ISIS period; elections (May); 5-month government formation. IMF program
continues. Budget transparency improving marginally as crisis recedes. IBP
OBS 2019 will cover this period. Some budget documents beginning to be
published online.

2019.assessment:
IBP OBS 2019: Iraq score 20/100. Significant improvement from previous
surveys. Executive Budget Proposal published. Some in-year reports available.
But citizens budget, pre-budget statement not published. Legislature oversight
capacity limited. Still classified as minimal transparency.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership. Open Budget Survey 2019. Iraq.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 and oil price collapse create emergency fiscal conditions.
Parliament delays budget passage. IMF emergency financing. Kadhimi
government (May) attempts fiscal reforms. IBP OBS 2021 will cover
this period.

2021.assessment:
IBP OBS 2021: Iraq score 23/100. Slight improvement from 2019. Executive
Budget Proposal published; some audit reports available. No citizens budget
or pre-budget statement. Legislature oversight weak. Classified as minimal
transparency.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership. Open Budget Survey 2021. Iraq.

2022.assessment:
Political stalemate (10 months); 2022 budget not passed until April amid
crisis. Sudani government (October). Some fiscal improvements with oil
price recovery. IBP OBS 2023 will cover this period.

2023.assessment:
IBP OBS 2023: Iraq score 23/100. Unchanged from 2021. Sudani government
passes largest budget in Iraqi history (2023-2025 triennial). Some
improvements in budget process but public transparency unchanged.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership. Open Budget Survey 2023. Iraq.

2024.assessment:
Triennial budget (2023-2025) implementation year. Oil revenues above
projections. IBP OBS 2025 will assess this period. Budget transparency
likely unchanged at ~23/100 level.

2025.assessment:
Projection: Budget transparency likely remains in 20-25/100 range.
No major transparency reforms expected under current political system.
Triennial budget concludes this year.

1996.assessment: Iraq under Saddam Hussein in 1996 ranked at the very bottom of the global corruption distribution, with a WGI estimate of -1.60 and a percentile rank of 0.54. The Ba'athist state was characterized by elite patronage networks, systematic oil revenue diversion, and complete absence of independent oversight mechanisms. Corruption was institutionalized as a tool of political control.

1998.assessment: Iraq in 1998 under sanctions remained at the extreme low end of the global corruption distribution. The Oil-for-Food program, operational since late 1996, created new vectors for corruption and diversion, while the Ba'athist patronage system continued to dominate resource allocation. The estimate of -1.42 places Iraq in the bottom percentile globally.

2000.assessment: By 2000 Iraq ranked at the very bottom of the global corruption distribution with a percentile rank of 0.0. The late Saddam era was marked by systematic Oil-for-Food corruption, rentier patronage networks, and severe suppression of any accountability mechanisms. International sanctions further incentivized rent-seeking behavior within regime circles.

2002.assessment: In the final year before the US-led invasion, Iraq remained among the most corrupt states globally at -1.39. The Ba'athist system's deep entrenchment of patronage and Oil-for-Food diversion continued. The slight uptick from 2000 likely reflects methodological changes rather than genuine improvement.

2003.assessment: The 2003 estimate captures the transition year of the US-led invasion and collapse of the Ba'athist state. The WGI estimate of -1.21 with a wide confidence interval reflects high uncertainty: the Ba'athist corruption system was dismantled, but new forms of post-conflict corruption quickly emerged amid state collapse, looting, and CPA contracting irregularities.

2004.assessment: By 2004, under the Coalition Provisional Authority and early transitional governance, Iraq's corruption score worsened to -1.49 with a percentile rank of 1.48. Large-scale reconstruction contracting, weak oversight institutions, and the emergence of sectarian patronage networks all contributed to entrenched corruption in the post-Saddam order.

2005.assessment: Iraq's 2005 score of -1.38 with a slightly improved percentile of 4.39 reflects the first post-invasion elections and nascent constitutional process, but corruption remained pervasive. Sectarian allocation of ministerial posts created competing patronage networks, and security sector corruption was rampant amid ongoing insurgency.

2006.assessment: Amid sectarian civil conflict peaking in 2006, Iraq's corruption score deteriorated to -1.45 with a percentile of 1.46. Militias operating within government ministries, ghost soldiers on payrolls, and oil smuggling contributed to what Transparency International described as one of the most corrupt countries in the world.

2007.assessment: During the 2007 US surge period, Iraq's corruption score of -1.46 reflected continued systemic corruption despite improved security. The Nouri al-Maliki government was widely documented to use state resources for sectarian advantage, and Iraq ranked 178th out of 179 countries in Transparency International's 2007 CPI.

2008.assessment: Iraq's 2008 score of -1.46 shows no improvement despite increased security from the surge. As oil revenues rose, so did opportunities for graft; the expanded number of sources (8) from 2008 onward provides a somewhat more reliable estimate. Corruption in procurement and security forces remained among the worst globally.

2009.assessment: A modest improvement to -1.33 in 2009 coincided with the US-Iraq Status of Forces Agreement and increased Iraqi governmental sovereignty. Despite formal anti-corruption bodies established under the new constitutional framework, enforcement remained weak and political interference in anti-corruption institutions was documented extensively.

2010.assessment: Scoring -1.36 in 2010 after disputed parliamentary elections and a prolonged government formation period, Iraq's corruption worsened slightly. Rising oil revenues under Maliki's centralized control created new patronage opportunities. The Integrity Commission and inspectors general system faced persistent political obstruction.

2011.assessment: As US forces withdrew from Iraq in 2011, the score improved marginally to -1.26. However, the withdrawal removed external oversight mechanisms, and Maliki's government continued to consolidate power through patronage. Widespread protests earlier in 2011 had partly focused on corruption in public services.

2012.assessment: The 2012 score of -1.25 represents a slight plateau in corruption. While Iraq's percentile improved to 8.53, this partly reflects expansion of the country sample. Oil revenues at record highs coincided with documented theft and inflated government contracts, particularly in the electricity and defense sectors.

2013.assessment: Iraq's corruption score worsened slightly to -1.32 in 2013, the year before ISIS expansion revealed the extent to which military corruption had gutted effective defense capacity. Documented ghost soldiers and officers, bribery at checkpoints, and politically-motivated contracting were widespread.

2014.assessment: The 2014 ISIS takeover of Mosul exposed catastrophically corrupt military structures where officers had sold equipment and listed phantom soldiers. Under new PM Haider al-Abadi, anti-corruption rhetoric increased but structural change was limited. Iraq's score of -1.36 reflects continued pervasive corruption across state institutions.

2015.assessment: Iraq's 2015 score deteriorated to -1.43 amid the fiscal crisis caused by falling oil prices and ISIS military costs. Massive protests in Baghdad and southern cities in summer 2015 were explicitly focused on government corruption and service failures. Abadi's reform package faced parliamentary resistance from entrenched patronage interests.

2016.assessment: The 2016 score of -1.46 reflects continued deterioration during the Mosul campaign. Militia financing through state institutions, diversion of reconstruction funds, and the PMF (Popular Mobilization Forces) operating outside formal accountability frameworks all deepened systemic corruption.

2017.assessment: Following the defeat of ISIS in Mosul in 2017, Iraq's corruption score improved marginally to -1.43. Reconstruction planning began but was immediately shadowed by concerns about diversion of reconstruction funds. Haider al-Abadi's government publicly committed to anti-corruption measures in the lead-up to 2018 elections.

2018.assessment: Under new PM Adel Abdul Mahdi, corruption worsened in 2018 to -1.46. Major reconstruction contracts went to politically connected firms, and ports revenue theft became a major scandal. Iran-aligned parties' control of key ministries facilitated patronage networks that operated with near-total impunity.

2019.assessment: Mass protests from October 2019 (Tishreen movement) were driven primarily by anti-corruption grievances, with demonstrators demanding systemic accountability and denouncing the muhasasa (sectarian quota) system. The score of -1.39 with a percentile of 8.10 reflects marginal improvement but ongoing pervasive corruption.

2020.assessment: Iraq's 2020 score of -1.33 showed marginal improvement while the regime theft scandal ('heist of the century' - theft of ~$2.5 billion from the tax authority) was uncovered. Under PM Mustafa al-Kadhimi, targeted anti-corruption investigations began but systemic change remained elusive amid COVID-19 fiscal pressures.

2021.assessment: Iraq's 2021 score of -1.27 represents the best reading since 2012, though still pervasive corruption. The Kadhimi government continued selective prosecutions while the October 2021 parliamentary elections saw boycotts partly motivated by anti-corruption frustration. The 'theft of the century' scandal prosecution accelerated but major figures remained protected.

2022.assessment: A modest improvement to -1.21 in 2022 under PM Mohammed al-Sudani's new government, which took office in October. The government prioritized anti-corruption commitments in its political platform, and oil revenues surged with rising prices, providing both resources and temptation. Systemic corruption remained deeply embedded.

2023.assessment: Iraq's 2023 score worsened to -1.32 from -1.21, indicating deterioration under the Sudani government despite continued anti-corruption rhetoric. Investigations into the 'theft of the century' continued, but high-level prosecutions remained limited. Port and border revenues, customs, and public procurement remained major corruption vectors.

1988.assessment: Major oil producer. Ba'ath controls all oil revenues. Oil-for-weapons (post-1980 war debts). No EITI. Complete opacity.

1989.assessment: Iraq oil production. All revenues to Saddam regime. No transparency. EITI not founded.

1990.assessment: Kuwait invasion; oil field sabotage. UNSC sanctions begin (August 1990). Oil revenues halted by sanctions. No EITI.

1991.assessment: Gulf War; Kuwait oil fires. UN oil embargo. Iraq oil revenues near zero from sanctions. No EITI framework.

1992.assessment: UN sanctions. No oil exports. Oil-for-Food not yet active. No extractive transparency.

1993.assessment: UN sanctions continue. No legitimate oil export. EITI not founded. Complete opacity.

1994.assessment: Oil-for-Food negotiations begin. No EITI. Iraqi oil revenue opaque.

1995.assessment: Oil-for-Food program approved (UNSCR 986). Implementation not yet begun. No EITI.

1996.assessment: Oil-for-Food begins. Iraq oil exports resume under UN supervision - but Saddam uses kickbacks. No EITI.

1997.assessment: Oil-for-Food operating. Iraq oil revenues partially visible through UN mechanism but surcharges hidden. No EITI.

1998.assessment: Oil-for-Food expanded. Iraq increases kickback scheme. Oil revenues partially visible via UN but opacity high. No EITI.

1999.assessment: Oil-for-Food maximum production. Saddam kickback/surcharge scheme growing. EITI not founded. No disclosure.

2000.assessment: Oil-for-Food peak; Saddam surcharges at maximum. No EITI (founded 2002). No disclosure.

2001.assessment: Pre-invasion. Oil-for-Food corruption continuing. EITI not yet founded. No extractive transparency.

2002.assessment:
Ba'athist Iraq: complete opacity in oil revenues. INOC (Iraq National Oil
Company) controlled by Saddam's family; Oil-for-Food corruption documented.
EITI not yet established globally (launched 2002 Johannesburg summit).
No revenue disclosure. Total opacity.

sources.citation: Volcker Committee. UN Independent Inquiry Committee into Oil-for-Food Program. 2005.

2003.assessment:
Regime collapse; Development Fund for Iraq (DFI) established under CPA
authority with IAMB oversight. Oil revenues managed under CPA with some
external monitoring (KPMG audit). First departure from total opacity.
Not EITI process but external oversight of oil revenues begins.

sources.citation: International Advisory and Monitoring Board for Iraq (IAMB). Audit Reports 2003.
RAND Corporation. Occupying Iraq: CPA History. 2009.

2004.assessment:
DFI continues with IAMB oversight. KPMG auditing oil revenues. Sovereignty
transferred (June); DFI transferred to Iraqi sovereignty with continuing
IAMB oversight. Not EITI but some external monitoring of oil revenues.
Minimal but more than pre-2003.

sources.citation: US General Accounting Office. Iraq Oil Revenue Accounting. 2004.

2005.assessment:
Iraq government begins exploring EITI candidacy under donor pressure.
New constitutional framework provides basis for resource management.
IAMB oversight continuing on DFI. Oil-for-Food inquiry (Volcker report
released 2005) exposes Ba'ath era opacity.

sources.citation: Volcker Committee. UN Independent Inquiry Committee Final Report. 2005.

2006.assessment:
Civil war limits institutional capacity for EITI preparation. Oil exports
continue but revenue management opaque due to security conditions. IAMB
oversight winding down. Iraq begins formal EITI candidacy process.

sources.citation: EITI Secretariat. Iraq EITI Candidacy Process. https://eiti.org/iraq

2007.assessment:
Iraq signs the EITI and becomes candidate country. Despite ongoing conflict,
EITI Multi-Stakeholder Group (MSG) established. Iraq Petroleum Revenue Watch
program supporting EITI implementation. First step toward formal extractive
transparency.

sources.citation: EITI Secretariat. Iraq EITI Candidacy 2007. https://eiti.org/iraq
Revenue Watch Institute. Iraq Petroleum Revenue Watch. 2007.

2008.assessment:
EITI candidate status. MSG operating. First EITI reconciliation report
being prepared for fiscal year 2007. Oil revenues from Iraq Petroleum
Company and international oil companies being reconciled. Security
challenges slow process but candidacy maintained.

2009.assessment:
EITI candidate. First reconciliation report published (covering FY2007).
International oil company contracts beginning under new licensing rounds.
MSG active. Provincial elections improve governance environment for EITI.
Candidacy in good standing.

sources.citation: Iraq EITI. First EITI Reconciliation Report (FY2007). 2009.

2010.assessment:
EITI candidate with active reporting. Annual reconciliation reports
covering IOC payments and government receipts. US drawdown does not
affect EITI process. MSG meetings regular. Candidacy progressing toward
compliance validation.

sources.citation: Iraq EITI. Annual Reconciliation Report FY2008. 2010.

2011.assessment:
Iraq close to completing EITI validation. Regular annual reports.
Oil production ramping up significantly after new contracts with IOCs
(BP, Shell, ExxonMobil). Large oil revenues being tracked through
EITI. MSG active. Validation in progress.

2012.assessment:
Iraq declared EITI Compliant in 2012. Major milestone for major oil exporter.
Annual EITI reports published covering all major oil and gas companies
operating in Iraq (BP, Shell, Lukoil, ExxonMobil, CNOOC, etc.). One of
highest-volume EITI reporting countries globally.

sources.citation: EITI Secretariat. Iraq EITI Compliant Status 2012. https://eiti.org/iraq

2013.assessment:
EITI Compliant status maintained. New EITI Standard 2013 adopted globally.
Iraq working toward compliance with expanded standard. Annual EITI reports
published; covers one of world's largest oil sectors. Corrective actions
identified for full compliance with new standard.

2014.assessment:
ISIS conquers Mosul (June) but does not affect EITI compliance in
government-controlled areas. EITI reports cover Kurdistan Region of Iraq
(KRG) separately. ISIS oil smuggling outside any EITI framework.
Government-controlled production maintains EITI reporting.

2015.assessment:
EITI compliant status maintained despite ongoing ISIS conflict. Iraq
EITI covers world-record oil production (4+ million bpd). ISIS oil
revenues outside framework but government-controlled production
fully covered. Fiscal crisis (oil price collapse) does not affect EITI.

2016.assessment:
EITI compliant. Mosul offensive begins (October). ISIS oil revenues collapsing
as territory shrinks. Government EITI reporting continues normally.
Baghdad-Kurdistan oil revenue disputes not yet resolved; both report
separately to EITI.

2017.assessment:
EITI validation (2017) finds Iraq making meaningful progress. Score
upgraded from corrective actions to meaningful progress. ISIS territorial
defeat enables full EITI coverage restoration in formerly-ISIS areas.
Iraq EITI among most comprehensive globally by volume.

sources.citation: EITI Secretariat. Iraq EITI Validation 2017. Meaningful Progress.

2018.assessment:
EITI meaningful progress status. Post-ISIS reconstruction includes
expanded EITI coverage in formerly-ISIS areas. Kurdistan-Baghdad oil
dispute EITI dimension: both maintain EITI reporting. Iraq EITI
reports cover massive oil sector (4+ million bpd).

2019.assessment:
EITI meaningful progress. Tishreen protests partly driven by oil revenue
mismanagement complaints, highlighting gap between EITI disclosure and
actual accountability. Revenue disclosure exists but accountability weak.

sources.citation: Natural Resource Governance Institute. Iraq Resource Governance Index. 2019.

2020.assessment:
EITI meaningful progress. Oil price collapse and COVID-19 emergency reduce
oil revenues. EITI reporting continues. Some delays in reporting due to
COVID-19 but MSG remains active.

2021.assessment:
EITI meaningful progress status maintained. Iraq EITI among world's
largest by volume. New validation cycle beginning. Beneficial ownership
disclosure requirements being addressed.

2022.assessment:
EITI meaningful progress. Sudani government (October) supportive of EITI.
Oil revenues surge with high prices and production. MSG active. Annual
reports covering massive oil sector published.

2023.assessment:
EITI meaningful progress. Iraq passes triennial budget partly based on
transparent oil revenue projections. EITI disclosures inform budget
debates. Validation cycle ongoing. Strong EITI performance relative
to regional peers.

2024.assessment:
EITI meaningful progress expected to be maintained. New EITI validation
cycle results anticipated. High oil production and prices continue.
Iraq remains among the world's most EITI-transparent oil producers.

2025.assessment:
Projection: EITI meaningful progress or potentially satisfactory. Iraq
has demonstrated sustained EITI commitment since 2007. Likely to maintain
or improve status.

Courts subordinate to Ba'ath Party. RCC (Revolutionary Command Council) decrees have force of law. WJP not established. No accessible legal framework.

Courts subordinate to Ba'ath Party. RCC (Revolutionary Command Council) decrees have force of law. WJP not established. No accessible legal framework.

2002.assessment:
Ba'athist Iraq: legal transparency non-existent. Laws published by
Ba'ath party controlled Official Gazette but enforcement entirely
arbitrary. Presidential decrees override all laws; judicial system
completely politicized. No rule of law; rule by decree and terror.

sources.citation: Makiya, Kanan. Republic of Fear. University of California Press, 1998.

2003.assessment:
Regime collapse eliminates existing (dysfunctional) legal framework. CPA
issues orders and regulations as new legal framework. Ba'ath party laws
repealed selectively. No accessible unified legal framework. CPA regulations
not always published or accessible. Chaotic legal environment.

sources.citation: Bremer, L. Paul. My Year in Iraq. Simon & Schuster, 2006.
RAND Corporation. Occupying Iraq: CPA History. 2009.

2004.assessment:
Transitional Administrative Law (TAL) provides legal framework for transition.
Sovereignty transferred (June). New legal codes being drafted. Courts
beginning to function but highly insecure. Laws not systematically published
or accessible. Partial but chaotic legal framework.

sources.citation: Diamond, Larry. Squandered Victory. Times Books, 2005.

2005.assessment:
New constitution approved (October). Formal legal framework being developed.
Courts extremely limited by insecurity. Laws passed by transitional
legislature. Law not systematically accessible to public. Difficult
enforcement environment.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2005. Country report Iraq.

2006.assessment:
Civil war peak; legal framework exists on paper but enforcement completely
absent in many areas. Sectarian death squads operate outside any law.
Courts function in some areas; completely absent in others. Legal system
highly arbitrary.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom in the World 2006. Country report Iraq.

2007.assessment:
Surge begins to improve security; some legal institutions beginning to
function more regularly. Courts operating in Baghdad and relatively secure
areas. Significant gaps remain; arbitrary enforcement common. Legal texts
not systematically accessible.

sources.citation: World Justice Project. Rule of Law Index (pre-publication data). 2008.

2008.assessment:
WJP begins covering Iraq. Scores very low (~20-25/100 for open government
factor). SOFA negotiated (November) as major legal document. Some legal
reforms underway. Courts more functional but highly politicized.
Arbitrary enforcement continues.

sources.citation: World Justice Project. Rule of Law Index 2011 (first formal publication). Iraq data from 2008 onwards.

2009.assessment:
Some improvement in rule of law indicators. Provincial elections held
lawfully. Courts more active but backlogs massive. Legal publications
improving marginally. WJP factor 3 (open government) ~22-25/100.

sources.citation: World Justice Project. Rule of Law Index historical data. Iraq.

2010.assessment:
Legal framework developing but Maliki weaponizes judiciary against rivals.
8-month government formation stalemate reveals limits of legal dispute
resolution. Courts partly politicized. WJP factor 3 ~22-25/100.

sources.citation: World Justice Project. Rule of Law Index historical data. Iraq.

2011.assessment:
WJP Rule of Law Index 2011: Iraq factor 3 (open government) ~22/100.
Among lowest scoring globally. Laws accessible only partially; government
information not proactively published; complaint mechanisms weak. Arbitrary
enforcement common. Maliki uses judiciary against opponents.

sources.citation: World Justice Project. Rule of Law Index 2011. Iraq.

2012.assessment:
WJP 2012: Iraq open government factor ~23/100. Marginal improvement.
Laws somewhat more accessible through official gazette online. Maliki
government continues restricting political opposition through judiciary.
Difficult enforcement environment.

sources.citation: World Justice Project. Rule of Law Index 2012. Iraq.

2013.assessment:
WJP 2014 (covering ~2013): Iraq open government ~24/100. ISIS resurgence
and Sunni protest crackdowns undermine rule of law. Arbitrary enforcement
increasing. Hawija crackdown outside any legal framework.

sources.citation: World Justice Project. Rule of Law Index 2014. Iraq.

2014.assessment:
WJP 2015 (covering ~2014): Iraq open government ~22/100. ISIS takeover
of Mosul collapses legal system in one-third of country. In government
areas: Abadi replaces Maliki; legal environment modestly better. But
ISIS areas: complete absence of any legitimate legal framework.

sources.citation: World Justice Project. Rule of Law Index 2015. Iraq.

2015.assessment:
WJP 2016 (covering ~2015): Iraq open government ~22/100. ISIS at territorial
peak. Legal transparency severely limited by conflict. In ISIS areas:
sharia law enforced without accountability. In government areas: laws
poorly accessible.

sources.citation: World Justice Project. Rule of Law Index 2016. Iraq.

2016.assessment:
WJP 2017 (covering ~2016): Iraq open government ~24/100. ISIS territory
shrinking; Mosul offensive begins (October). Some legal recovery in liberated
areas. Government legal framework marginally more accessible.

sources.citation: World Justice Project. Rule of Law Index 2017-18. Iraq.

2017.assessment:
WJP 2017-18 ~25/100. ISIS territorial defeat enables legal recovery in
former-ISIS areas. Mosul courts reopening. Kurdistan independence referendum
crisis does not significantly affect open government scoring. Partial legal
transparency with significant gaps.

sources.citation: World Justice Project. Rule of Law Index 2017-18. Iraq.

2018.assessment:
WJP 2019 ~26/100. Continued recovery in legal transparency. Some laws
more accessible; complaint mechanisms improving. Abdul-Mahdi government
attempts some legal reforms. Still ranked very low globally.

sources.citation: World Justice Project. Rule of Law Index 2019. Iraq.

2019.assessment:
WJP 2020 ~27/100. Tishreen protests partly about rule of law failures.
Internet shutdowns during protests undermine open government. PM Abdul-Mahdi
resigns amid protests. Legal access marginally improving but protest
crackdown reveals arbitrary enforcement.

sources.citation: World Justice Project. Rule of Law Index 2020. Iraq.

2020.assessment:
WJP 2020 ~27/100. COVID-19 emergency measures restrict some transparency.
Kadhimi government (May) attempts rule of law improvements. Continued
arbitrary enforcement by PMF outside legal constraints. Partial legal
transparency.

sources.citation: World Justice Project. Rule of Law Index 2020. Iraq.

2021.assessment:
WJP 2021 ~28/100. Marginal improvements in open government. Kadhimi
government publishes some legal documents more systematically. Still
ranked among lowest globally. Partial but inadequate legal transparency.

sources.citation: World Justice Project. Rule of Law Index 2021. Iraq.

2022.assessment:
WJP 2022 ~27/100. Political stalemate and Green Zone crisis do not
significantly affect legal transparency score. Sudani government
(October) begins with some reform commitments. Legal framework broadly
unchanged.

sources.citation: World Justice Project. Rule of Law Index 2022. Iraq.

2023.assessment:
WJP 2023 ~28/100. Sudani government passes record budget with some
transparency provisions. Legal framework marginally more accessible.
Anti-LGBTQ law restricts certain information. Still very low by global
standards.

sources.citation: World Justice Project. Rule of Law Index 2023. Iraq.

2024.assessment:
WJP 2024 data expected. Legal transparency likely maintained around 27-29/100.
Some improvements in government information publication. PMF still operates
outside legal constraints. Partial transparency with significant gaps.

sources.citation: World Justice Project. Rule of Law Index 2024. Iraq.

2025.assessment:
Projection: Legal transparency likely to remain around 27-30/100 (WJP).
No major rule of law reform expected without fundamental political change.
Partial legal transparency with significant gaps continuing.

sources.citation: World Justice Project. Rule of Law Index 2025. Iraq.

1988.assessment: Anfal campaign; journalists covering Kurdish genocide executed. Absolute state control.

1989.assessment: Ba'ath media monopoly. No independent press. CPJ: Iraq most dangerous for journalists.

1990.assessment: Kuwait invasion; wartime censorship. All media state-controlled. Gulf War propaganda.

1991.assessment: Gulf War. State media only. Allied bombing of media infrastructure. Post-war uprisings suppressed.

1992.assessment: Post-war repression. Shia/Kurdish uprisings covered up. No independent media.

1993.assessment: UN sanctions deepening. State media only. Critical journalists imprisoned/executed.

1994.assessment: Sanctions deepening. Absolute state media control. No independent press.

1995.assessment: Oil for Food program; state controls information. No independent media.

1996.assessment: Oil for Food begins. Baghdad media: total Ba'ath control. No press freedom.

1997.assessment: State media sole source of information. CPJ: Iraq worst for journalists globally.

1998.assessment: Operation Desert Fox bombing (December 1998). State media war propaganda. No independent press.

1999.assessment: Saddam Hussein absolute control. State television (Al-Iraqiyya) only. No press freedom.

2000.assessment: Saddam era. State monopoly. Uday Hussein controls Babel newspaper and Shabaab TV. No freedom.

2001.assessment: Post-9/11; Saddam propaganda. State media only. No independent journalism. Journalists at extreme risk.

2002.assessment:
Ba'athist Iraq: total state control of all media. No independent press.
All newspapers and broadcast media state-owned and controlled. Foreign
journalists require regime permission; access tightly restricted. CPJ
documents executions of journalists. No freedom of press.

sources.citation: Committee to Protect Journalists. Iraq Country Reports. 2002.
Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2002 (first year).

2003.assessment:
Under Ba'ath until April: total press suppression. Post-invasion: explosion
of new media (hundreds of new newspapers and TV stations). But journalists
heavily targeted by insurgents and US forces; CPJ documents dozens of
journalist deaths. RSF score ~78 (raw bad score) reflecting dangerous
conditions. Very serious.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2003.
Committee to Protect Journalists. Iraq: Deadly Transition. 2003.

2004.assessment:
Iraq becomes deadliest country for journalists globally. Journalists killed
by insurgents, US military, and militias. New media landscape (600+ new
outlets) but extreme danger. Some outlets closed by CPA for incitement.
RSF raw ~80; very serious.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2004.
Committee to Protect Journalists. Iraq Annual Report 2004.

2005.assessment:
Iraq remains most dangerous country for journalists worldwide. Kidnappings,
targeted killings of journalists. Civil war conditions make reporting
extremely dangerous. Sectarian media developing alongside independent press.
Very serious environment. RSF raw ~81.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2005.
Committee to Protect Journalists. Iraq Annual Report 2005.

2006.assessment:
Civil war peak; journalists face extreme danger from all sides. Sectarian
militias target journalists of opposing sect. Over 30 journalists killed in
2006. Sectarian media proliferates; independent journalism extremely
dangerous. RSF raw ~83.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2006.
Committee to Protect Journalists. Iraq Annual Report 2006.

2007.assessment:
Still among the most dangerous countries for journalists. Surge begins
to reduce violence but press freedom remains very serious. Journalist
killings continue. Embedded journalism dominates foreign coverage.
RSF raw ~82.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2007.

2008.assessment:
Violence declining; some improvement in journalist safety. Still very
serious conditions; press freedom remains highly constrained. Maliki
government begins restricting critical media. RSF raw ~79.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2008.

2009.assessment:
Continued improvement in security but Maliki government increasingly
hostile to critical media. Some journalists arrested. Press freedom still
very serious; Iraq remains one of most dangerous countries for journalists
despite improved security. RSF raw ~76.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2009.

2010.assessment:
Elections year; some media pluralism around elections. Maliki government
retains critical media hostility. Journalists face threats from both
government and remnant armed groups. RSF raw ~75. Very serious.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2010.

2011.assessment:
US withdrawal; Maliki consolidates press repression. Arab Spring protests
covered but journalists harassed. Reporters covering government protests
face arrest. RSF raw ~74. Very serious.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2011.

2012.assessment:
Maliki government increasingly hostile to critical media. Journalists
arrested for covering protests. Some media outlets raided. Still among
more dangerous countries for journalism. RSF raw ~72. Very serious.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2012.

2013.assessment:
Rising violence with ISIS resurgence. Journalists covering Sunni protests
arrested. Hawija coverage restricted. Press freedom constrained by both
government actions and rising insurgent threats. RSF raw ~73. Very serious.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2013.

2014.assessment:
ISIS conquers Mosul and major areas; complete suppression of all media
in ISIS-controlled territories. Journalists killed, expelled, or fled
from ISIS areas. James Foley and other journalists executed by ISIS.
Sharp deterioration in press freedom. RSF raw ~80.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2014.

2015.assessment:
ISIS controls one-third of Iraq with total press suppression. Government
areas: journalists face threats from government and militias. War reporting
extremely dangerous. RSF raw ~82.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2015.

2016.assessment:
Mosul offensive begins (October); embedded media only in military operations.
ISIS territory shrinking but still major press suppression there. Government
areas: hostile to critical media. RSF raw ~81.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2016.

2017.assessment:
Mosul liberated (July); ISIS territorial defeat. Media no longer under
ISIS control in former territories. Post-liberation: some improvement in
access. But PMF and government still hostile to critical media. Kurdistan
press relatively freer. RSF raw ~78.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2017.

2018.assessment:
Post-ISIS period; some improvement in press freedom conditions. Government
still restricts critical media; journalists covering Basra protests (September)
attacked. RSF raw ~72. Very serious but improving.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2018.

2019.assessment:
Tishreen protest crackdown; journalists covering protests targeted by
security forces and PMF. Internet shutdowns. Some journalists killed.
Press freedom deteriorates around protest period. RSF raw ~73.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2019.
Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2020.

2021.assessment:
Continued journalist assassinations; elections year with some media
coverage. Press conditions improving marginally but very serious conditions
persist. Activist journalists remain at risk from armed groups.
RSF raw ~72.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2021.

2022.assessment:
RSF 2022 methodology change (100-point scale); Iraq scores in difficult
to very serious range. Green Zone political crisis does not significantly
affect press freedom. Journalists still face threats. RSF raw ~71.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2022.

2023.assessment:
Press conditions marginally improving; Sudani government somewhat less
repressive than previous governments. But journalists still face threats.
Anti-LGBTQ law restricts reporting on certain topics. RSF raw ~69.
Classified as difficult to very serious.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2023.

2024.assessment:
Continued improvement in press freedom ranking but still difficult conditions.
US-Iran tensions around Gaza affect some journalists. Anti-LGBTQ law
constrains certain reporting. RSF raw ~68. Classified as difficult.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2024.

2025.assessment:
Projection: Press freedom likely to remain in difficult to very serious
range. No major improvement expected. Journalists continue to face threats
from armed groups and government.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. Press Freedom Index 2025.

1988.assessment: Saddam era 1988. Central Statistical Organization (CSO) exists but data highly politicized. Sanctions era: UN and international organizations estimate population/economic data from outside. No independent verification. WB SCI not established and not applicable. Data unreliable.

2003.assessment:
Regime collapse destroys statistical system. Central Statistical Organization
(CSO) looted and partially destroyed. No functioning national statistics
during CPA period. Ba'athist era data unreliable/manipulated. Statistical
system essentially needs rebuilding from scratch.

sources.citation: World Bank. Iraq Statistical System Rebuilding Report. 2004.

2004.assessment:
CSO rebuilding begins with donor support. No national census possible under
security conditions. Very limited statistical capacity. World Bank SCI does
not yet cover Iraq. Estimates and surveys replacing systematic data collection.

sources.citation: World Bank. Iraq Reconstruction: Early Progress. 2004.

2005.assessment:
CSO beginning to function with international support. Some household surveys
conducted (COSIT). Statistical capacity extremely low; conflict prevents
comprehensive data collection. Most economic data estimated.

sources.citation: COSIT (Central Organization for Statistics). Iraq. Annual Report 2005.

2006.assessment:
Civil war severely limits statistical capacity. No nationwide surveys
possible in conflict zones. COSIT limited to Baghdad and accessible areas.
World Bank SCI begins covering Iraq with very low scores (~10-15).

sources.citation: World Bank. Statistical Capacity Indicators for Iraq. https://datatopics.worldbank.org/statisticalcapacity/

2007.assessment:
WB SCI ~20/100. Civil war conditions severely limit statistical operations.
COSIT operating but with minimal coverage. Iraq Living Conditions Survey
(2006-2007) conducted with UNAMI support. Some household data emerging
but very limited national statistics.

sources.citation: World Bank. Statistical Capacity Indicator. Iraq 2007.

2008.assessment:
WB SCI ~30/100. Improving security allows expanded statistical operations.
COSIT developing more systematic data collection. IMF Article IV consultations
support statistical development. Still very poor by international standards
but improving.

sources.citation: World Bank. Statistical Capacity Indicator. Iraq 2008.

2009.assessment:
WB SCI ~37/100. Statistical capacity building supported by World Bank and
IMF. Population and housing census planned but not yet conducted. Some
sectoral statistics improving. Poor but trending upward.

sources.citation: World Bank. Statistical Capacity Indicator. Iraq 2009.

2010.assessment:
WB SCI ~41/100. Crosses into moderate range. Iraq Poverty Measurement survey
(2007) data being processed. COSIT developing administrative data systems.
Electoral data better than economic data. Moderate capacity emerging.

sources.citation: World Bank. Statistical Capacity Indicator. Iraq 2010.

2011.assessment:
WB SCI ~43/100. Statistical capacity continues to improve despite
political turbulence. US withdrawal reduces donor attention but Iraqi
capacity now more self-sustaining. Moderate level.

sources.citation: World Bank. Statistical Capacity Indicator. Iraq 2011.

2012.assessment:
WB SCI ~44/100. Household Socio-Economic Survey (IHSES 2012-2013) underway.
Administrative statistics improving. Maliki government does not prioritize
statistical transparency. Moderate capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank. Statistical Capacity Indicator. Iraq 2012.

2013.assessment:
WB SCI ~44/100. IHSES 2012-2013 household survey continues. Rising
security concerns from ISIS resurgence beginning to affect field surveys.
Statistical capacity stable but not improving.

sources.citation: World Bank. Statistical Capacity Indicator. Iraq 2013.

2014.assessment:
WB SCI ~36/100. ISIS conquest of Mosul (June) and one-third of Iraq
collapses statistical operations in northern and western Iraq. No surveys
possible in ISIS areas. COSIT operations severely curtailed. Sharp decline.

sources.citation: World Bank. Statistical Capacity Indicator. Iraq 2014.

2015.assessment:
WB SCI ~35/100. ISIS still controls major areas; no statistical coverage
of one-third of country. Emergency conditions dominate government priorities.
Continued degradation of statistical capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank. Statistical Capacity Indicator. Iraq 2015.

2016.assessment:
WB SCI ~35/100. Mosul offensive begins (October). ISIS territory slowly
shrinking but still restricting statistical operations. Fiscal crisis
(oil price collapse) reduces resources for statistics. Stable at poor level.

sources.citation: World Bank. Statistical Capacity Indicator. Iraq 2016.

2017.assessment:
WB SCI ~41/100. ISIS territorial defeat (December) allows statistical
recovery. Post-liberation household surveys begin in Mosul area. Donor
reconstruction funding includes statistical capacity building.
Recovery to moderate level.

sources.citation: World Bank. Statistical Capacity Indicator. Iraq 2017.

2018.assessment:
WB SCI ~46/100. Post-ISIS reconstruction includes statistical rebuilding.
Population census discussions (first since 1987). Some GDP and household
data improving. Moderate statistical capacity recovering.

sources.citation: World Bank. Statistical Capacity Indicator. Iraq 2018.

2019.assessment:
WB SCI ~50/100. Continued improvement in statistical capacity. Iraq
Household Socio-Economic Survey (2017) data published. Some sectoral
surveys improving. Tishreen protests do not significantly affect statistics.
Moderate capacity achieved.

sources.citation: World Bank. Statistical Capacity Indicator. Iraq 2019.

2020.assessment:
WB SCI ~47/100. COVID-19 disrupts field surveys. WB SCI methodology
changes may affect score. Slight decline but capacity broadly maintained.
Oil price collapse reduces funding for statistical services.

sources.citation: World Bank. Statistical Capacity Indicator. Iraq 2020.

2021.assessment:
WB SCI ~50/100. Recovery from COVID disruption. Statistical capacity
maintained at moderate level. Some improvement in national accounts.
No national census yet conducted (last 1987).

sources.citation: World Bank. Statistical Capacity Indicator. Iraq 2021.

2022.assessment:
WB SCI discontinued after 2021. Statistical capacity likely maintained
in moderate range. Sudani government passes large budget with some
statistical requirements. Oil revenue improvements fund some capacity
building.

2023.assessment:
Statistical capacity in moderate range. WB SCI no longer published.
Triennial budget includes provisions for census. Some improvements in
economic data. No national census conducted yet.

2024.assessment:
Statistical capacity maintained in moderate range. Census planning
continues but not yet conducted. Economic and demographic data improving
with oil recovery.

2025.assessment:
Projection: Moderate statistical capacity (~50/100 equivalent). No major
breakthrough expected without national census. Gradual improvement possible.
